EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one hour before transitioning to snow. A public report via social media estimated 2.0 inches of snowfall in Florence. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 31 mph across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The snow continued until 5 pm CST before transitioning to a light freezing drizzle or mist until precipitation ended at 8 pm CST. A public report of 4.0 inches of snowfall was observed 6 miles west southwest of Brandsville. CoCoRaHS observer MO-HL-30 approximately 11 miles west of West Plains reported 2.0 inches of snow. The UNO ASOS also reported 0.02 inches of flat ice accretion due to the freezing drizzle. Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and dangerous, with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds.   Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season. There were reports of two separate vehicle crashes involving a total of three vehicles and resulting in three reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of August 3rd, numerous thunderstorms developed across parts of eastern Missouri and west-central Illinois, triggered by strong surface heating ahead of a surging outflow boundary. These storms developed within a strongly unstable environment, featuring MLCAPE values ranging from 2500 to as much as 4000 J/kg and largely driven by the very warm and humid boundary layer. The initial round of storms struggled to become organized. Eventually cold pool interactions drove upscale growth into a more linear complex. Damaging winds, occasional large hail were reported as well as bursts of very heavy rain rates. By the evening of August 3rd, storms grew upscale, with additional rounds of thunderstorms developing upstream from this initial activity. The primary hazard transitioned from a damaging wind threat to a heavy rain and flash flooding threat, as storms were extremely efficient rain producers. Warm cloud depths were around 4.5km, along with anomalously high precipitable water values of 2 to 2.5 inches, and Corfidi vector orientations that supported westward back-building which enhanced the potential for locally heavy rain, both in terms of instantaneous rates (2+ inches per hour at times) and training of storms. By mid morning on August 4th, widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell, mainly along and south of I-70 in Missouri and along and east of I-55 in Illinois. Over the St. Louis metro area, as well as Fredericktown, MO, between 4 and 7 inches of rain fell. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty south winds occurred during the afternoon hours. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 47 mph at the Cape Girardeau Airport and 41 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport. The strong winds were ahead of a low pressure center and cold front over the central Plains states.

An upper level system moved into the region during the day on January 14th.  Widespread precipitation developed and spread across the region. Initially some locations had rain, but the rain quickly changed to all snow. It was a wet, heavy snow with snowfall rates up to an inch at times. A large portion of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois received over 6 inches of snow for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county with up to 7.5 inches reported in portions of Lewis County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved significantly across southeast Missouri during the month. The drought was no longer categorized as severe by the end of the month. Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. Wildfire danger decreased greatly by late in the month. Water levels on the Mississippi River increased, though they were below normal through the end of the month.

A cold front, that was draped northeast-southwest across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska/Kansas, moved south and entered the county warning area during the morning of February 1st.  Broad moist isentropic ascent and overrunning precipitation developed across the entire county warning area gradually through the late morning into the afternoon of February 1st, with the baroclinic zone initially serving as the focus for rain. This first round of precipitation stayed nearly all rain, but snow did begin to mix with rain in northern Missouri and west-central Illinois during the afternoon hours of February 1st as colder air advanced south behind the front.  The cold air became more established in the northwest half of the CWA with wet snow and a snowfall rate approaching 1/hr across parts of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois north of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of February 2nd.  The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures dropped during the late evening hours, rain changed over to freezing rain. By midnight on February 2nd, around one to two tenths of an inch of icing was reported across Montgomery County. Then the liquid precipitation changed over to snow. Between eight to ten inches of snow fell across Montgomery County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system developed with a cold front moving slowly south across the region. A round of sleet and freezing rain developed north of the boundary across portions of central/northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Then the mixed precipitation changed over to all snow before coming to an end. Some locations reported between half an inch and 1.5 inches of sleet, as well as up to 11 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county with storm total snowfall between 6 and 10.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county with storm total snowfall up to 8 inches.

The cold air became more established in the northwest half of the CWA with wet snow and a snowfall rate approaching 1/hr across parts of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois north of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of February 2nd.  The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of precipitation with this system. The first round was rain changing to a mix of sleet and snow, then another round of heavy snow. Up to half an inch of sleet was reported in the far northwestern portions of Madison County during the morning hours of February 2nd. With the second round of precipitation, storm total snowfall up to 6 inches was reported in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system developed with a cold front moving slowly south across the region. A round of sleet and freezing rain developed north of the boundary across portions of central/northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Then the mixed precipitation changed over to all snow before coming to an end. Some locations reported between half an inch and 1.5 inches of sleet, as well as up to 11 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county with storm total snowfall up to 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved significantly across southeast Missouri during the month. The drought was no longer categorized as severe by the end of the month. Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. Wildfire danger decreased greatly by late in the month. Water levels on the Mississippi River increased, though they were below normal through the end of the month.

A mulch fire initiated late morning on October 23rd at a compost facility just south of Interstate 470 in far south Kansas City, MO (just west of the Lee's Summit city limit). Smoke from the fire shut down I-470 for a couple hours and an apartment complex was asked to evacuate. The fire was mostly contained by that evening but continued to smolder for days, even briefly re-igniting on October 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire initiated within a compost pile at Missouri Organic Recycling at 10301 Raytown Road in south Kansas City around 11 AM . This pile was nearly 3 stories tall and consisted of tree limbs, grass clippings, and other yard waste. Weather conditions around ignition time were a temperature of 75, relative humidity of 55 percent, and winds out of the south as 19 knots gusting to 34 knots. Smoke from the fire shut down Interstate 470 for a couple hours. The fire was also able to jump I-470 due to the strong southerly winds and burned an abandoned house, barn, and multiple garages north of I-470.  Residents of View High Lake Apartments were evacuated as a precaution, but the fire was contained before threatening any dwellings. The fire continued to smolder for several days, and even briefly re-ignited on October 26th. Ten fire departments and 4 law enforcement agencies responded to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of 10/21, a wildfire burned 10 to 15 acres near Bucker, MO. Weather conditions were favorable for fire ignition and spread that afternoon with very warm temperatures, very low humidity, and strong southwesterly wind gusts. Two firefighters were injured (one critically) fighting the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An illegal outdoor burn started a wild/grass fire at 31000 E Blue Mills Road in Buckner, MO around 2 pm CDT on 10/21. The fire only burned about 15 acres, but 2 firefighters were injured and their brush truck was destroyed battling the fire. One of these firefighters was critically injured and had to be hospitalized.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across north central and northeastern Missouri. On October 4th, the drought monitor showed D2 drought building into eastern Adair County, with this expanding further to include most of the southern two thirds of the county by mid October and continuing through the remainder of the month.

Around a deep 500 mb low centered just north of the Great Lakes, a shortwave pivoted east-southeast and intensified as it moved across the the Ohio Valley region. Temperatures fell well below freezing ahead of it, due to a cold front that swept through the previous day. Temperatures in the upper teens to lower 20's resulted in a dry, fluffy snow. These cold temperatures also allowed snow to quickly accumulate on roads. A major multi-vehicle accident on I-55 near Portageville in New Madrid County kept the interstate closed for several hours for crash clean up. Missouri State Highway Patrol Troop E reported over 100 minor wrecks. Snowfall totals were highest in the Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky, where about four inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts averaged around an inch across the rest of southeast Missouri.

A storm system brought widespread snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. The precipitation began as rain but changed over to snow from west to east between midnight and 8 am, with the snow continuing much of the day and into the evening hours in a few locations. There was quite a spread in accumulations across the area, but a heavy band of snow did develop and extended from the Arkansas border in southern Taney County into Douglas and Wright counties where 7 to 8 inches of snow fell. Outside of this area, snowfall ranged from 1 to 6 inches. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported, with most coming from areas within the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county during the early morning hours of January 15th, with the snow then continuing through the day and into the evening hours. Widespread snow amounts between 4 and 8 inches fell across the county. A narrow band of heavier snow dumped 7 to 8 inches. The Cooperative Observer 2 miles northeast of Ridgedale at the Branson Airport reported a measured storm total of 8.0 inches, while a public report in Ridgedale also reported a measured total snowfall of 8.0 inches. Meanwhile, a measured 4.0 inches of total snowfall was reported in Branson and 5.0 inches 1 mile northwest of Kirbyville. Roads became snow covered and slippery with numerous slide-offs and accidents being reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak storm system dropped through the Missouri Ozarks during the morning hours of January 6, 2022. The snow was light and fluffy and brought widespread accumulations between one half inch and an inch and a half. Despite the light accumulations, this was really the first snowfall of the season after a mild end to 2021. Roads did become snow covered and slippery with a number of slide offs. In addition, most of the schools were closed both Thursday January 6 and Friday January 7 due to the icy roads and cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager in Macks Creek reported 1.5 inches of total snowfall. Roads were snow covered and slippery with schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions gradually expanded and worsened across southeast Missouri through the last week of the month. The drought was classified as extreme across the southeast corner of the state, from Cape Girardeau and Dexter east to the Mississippi River, including New Madrid. The remainder of southeast Missouri was classified as severe drought. Most crops were in the process of being harvested, so agricultural impacts were very limited. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which was likely to become in short supply in the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. The wildfire danger rose to critical levels on a few days where windy conditions coincided with very low humidity. Bans on outdoor burning were imposed in much of the region. Some locally beneficial rain arrived at the end of the month. Even so, most locations were still 3 to over 6 inches below normal on rainfall since September 1.  One of the most significant impacts was very low water levels on the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, nearly forcing the river transportation industry to a halt. Dredging helped restore navigation channels to a depth sufficiently deep for river traffic to resume in a limited capacity. On Saturday, October 15, 2022, the Mississippi River at New Madrid, MO, fell to -5.45 feet. This was the lowest observed river stage on record at New Madrid, where observations began in 1879.

Dredging helped restore navigation channels to a depth sufficiently deep for river traffic to resume in a limited capacity. On Saturday, October 15, 2022, the Mississippi River at New Madrid, MO, fell to -5.45 feet. This was the lowest observed river stage on record at New Madrid, where observations began in 1879.

Drought conditions gradually expanded and worsened across southeast Missouri through the last week of the month. The drought was classified as extreme across the southeast corner of the state, from Cape Girardeau and Dexter east to the Mississippi River, including New Madrid. The remainder of southeast Missouri was classified as severe drought. Most crops were in the process of being harvested, so agricultural impacts were very limited. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which was likely to become in short supply in the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. The wildfire danger rose to critical levels on a few days where windy conditions coincided with very low humidity. Bans on outdoor burning were imposed in much of the region. Some locally beneficial rain arrived at the end of the month. Even so, most locations were still 3 to over 6 inches below normal on rainfall since September 1.  One of the most significant impacts was very low water levels on the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, nearly forcing the river transportation industry to a halt. Dredging helped restore navigation channels to a depth sufficiently deep for river traffic to resume in a limited capacity. On Saturday, October 15, 2022, the Mississippi River at New Madrid, MO, fell to -5.45 feet. This was the lowest observed river stage on record at New Madrid, where observations began in 1879.

A storm system brought widespread snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. The precipitation began as rain but changed over to snow from west to east between midnight and 8 am, with the snow continuing much of the day and into the evening hours in a few locations. There was quite a spread in accumulations across the area, but a heavy band of snow did develop and extended from the Arkansas border in southern Taney County into Douglas and Wright counties where 7 to 8 inches of snow fell. Outside of this area, snowfall ranged from 1 to 6 inches. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported, with most coming from areas within the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county during the early morning hours of January 15th, with the snow then continuing through the day and into the evening hours. Widespread snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches fell across the county. There was a  public report of an estimated 6.0 inches of total snowfall in Branson West, while another public report of a estimated 5.0 inches was received from Cape Fair.  Roads became snow covered and slippery with numerous slide-offs and accidents being reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county during the pre dawn hours of January 15th, with the snow then continuing through much of the day. Widespread snow amounts between 2 and 6 inches fell across the county, with the heavier accumulations across the far western areas.  A measured 6.0 inches of snowfall was reported by the public in Rocky Comfort. Roads became snow covered and slippery across mainly the eastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the region. As cold air filtered into the region rain changed to snow across northern sections of the Mid-South. Around an inch fell over parts of Clay County, Arkansas, and Dunklin County, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one inch of snow fell over northern Dunklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-South on January 6th, 2022. Cold air filtered in behind the front. A surface low pressure system moved along the front providing enough moisture to result in two to four inches falling across the Missouri Bootheel. This caused dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3.5 inches of snow fell causing dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak storm system dropped through the Missouri Ozarks during the morning hours of January 6, 2022. The snow was light and fluffy and brought widespread accumulations between one half inch and an inch and a half. Despite the light accumulations, this was really the first snowfall of the season after a mild end to 2021. Roads did become snow covered and slippery with a number of slide offs. In addition, most of the schools were closed both Thursday January 6 and Friday January 7 due to the icy roads and cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report via Social Media of a measured 1.3 inches of total snowfall in El Dorado Springs. Roads were snow covered and slippery and schools closed for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around a deep 500 mb low centered just north of the Great Lakes, a shortwave pivoted east-southeast and intensified as it moved across the the Ohio Valley region. Temperatures fell well below freezing ahead of it, due to a cold front that swept through the previous day. Temperatures in the upper teens to lower 20's resulted in a dry, fluffy snow. These cold temperatures also allowed snow to quickly accumulate on roads. A major multi-vehicle accident on I-55 near Portageville in New Madrid County kept the interstate closed for several hours for crash clean up. Missouri State Highway Patrol Troop E reported over 100 minor wrecks. Snowfall totals were highest in the Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky, where about four inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts averaged around an inch across the rest of southeast Missouri.

A strong winter storm impacted much of southeast Iowa, western Illinois, and northeast Missouri. Widespread snowfall ranging between 5 and 10 inches of snow was reported in the hardest hit areas, with 1 to 4 inches surrounding and gusty winds to follow. This event started and ended as a snow event, as cold air was in place prior. With the gusty winds, blowing and drifting snow was observed, reducing visibilities and making it difficult to measure the snowfall. Just as this storm was moving out, very cold temperatures moved in, with wind chills between -15 to -30 felt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter, COOP, and public reports of 4-6 inches of snow fell across portions of Scotland County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 2nd. The COOP observer in Memphis reported a storm total snow of 6.3 inches.

With the gusty winds, blowing and drifting snow was observed, reducing visibilities and making it difficult to measure the snowfall. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter and public reports of 4-6 inches of snow fell across portions of Clark County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 2nd. The highest report was 5 inches received, but was ruled towards winter storm verification because of the combined impacts of other elements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of inches of snow fell in parts of the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. Most of the snow fell in a short time span, at an average rate of 1 inch per hour. The snow fell on the north side of a large precipitation shield wrapping around a surface low across the lower Mississippi Valley. Further support for precipitation was provided by a well-defined low in the upper levels of the atmosphere over the southern Plains. Two inches of snow was reported between Ellsinore and Van Buren in Carter County. A number of vehicles slid off roads in Wayne County.

Around a deep 500 mb low centered just north of the Great Lakes, a shortwave pivoted east-southeast and intensified as it moved across the the Ohio Valley region. Temperatures fell well below freezing ahead of it, due to a cold front that swept through the previous day. Temperatures in the upper teens to lower 20's resulted in a dry, fluffy snow. These cold temperatures also allowed snow to quickly accumulate on roads. A major multi-vehicle accident on I-55 near Portageville in New Madrid County kept the interstate closed for several hours for crash clean up. Missouri State Highway Patrol Troop E reported over 100 minor wrecks. Snowfall totals were highest in the Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky, where about four inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts averaged around an inch across the rest of southeast Missouri.

A storm system brought widespread snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. The precipitation began as rain but changed over to snow from west to east between midnight and 8 am, with the snow continuing much of the day and into the evening hours in a few locations. There was quite a spread in accumulations across the area, but a heavy band of snow did develop and extended from the Arkansas border in southern Taney County into Douglas and Wright counties where 7 to 8 inches of snow fell. Outside of this area, snowfall ranged from 1 to 6 inches. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported, with most coming from areas within the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow during the early morning hours of January 15th. Widespread snowfall of 3 to 5 inches was reported across the county, with the highest amounts across far southeast Barry County, where CoCoRaHS station MO-BY-25 located 5 miles south southeast of Cassville reported a measured 4.1 inches of storm total snowfall. A public report of 3.5 inches of measured total snowfall was received from Monett. Numerous slide offs were reported, one involving a semi-truck on Highway 39 just south of Kings Harbor Resort.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to all snow during the early morning hours of January 15th. Widespread snowfall amounts between 2 and 5 inches occurred across the county, with the highest amounts across the far southeastern sections of the county. An amateur radio operator reported a measured 2.8 inches of total snowfall at Highway 14 and main street in Nixa. Numerous slide off were reported as roads became snow covered and slippery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Friday afternoon - early Saturday morning.  This snow impacted the evening commute causing hazardous travel, especially between Des Moines and Iowa City on Interstate 80 and along I-380 between Iowa City and Cedar Rapids.  The highest snow totals between 8 and 12 inches were common roughly along a line from Des Moines to Keokuk.  Snow amounts between 4-7 inches were found across much of eastern Iowa towards west central Illinois.  Lesser amounts of 1-3 inches were reported in northern and north central Illinois.  ||Illinois, Iowa, and Missouri DOT reported slick and snow covered roads through much of the event.  Winds between 20-30 mph were also seen causing some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter, COOP, and public reports of 4-7 inches of snow fell across portions of Scotland County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 15th. The highest snowfall report from the event was 7 inches in Memphis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits from the summer/early fall months continued into October, with severe drought (D2) continuing and even expanding into extreme drought (D3) for much of the Interstate 70/Missouri River corridor in Missouri and Kansas per the UNL US Drought Monitor. These drought conditions yielded a fairly active fire weather season in October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits continued into October with severe to extreme drought persisting through the month. Saline County began October almost entirely within D2 with D3 drought building in over the southern half of the county by October 4th. By the end of October, all of Saline County was either in D2 or D3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of October 14th, a grass fire burned 40 to 50 acres and destroyed a home, garage, and farm equipment. Weather conditions around ignition time was a temperature of 70 degrees, relative humidity of 23 percent, and winds out of the WSW around 15 knots gusting to 27 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire began around 1 pm CDT on 10/14 at 16131 Drake Road, La Monte, MO 65337. The fire burned 40 to 50 acres and destroyed a house, garage, and farm equipment before the fire was contained. Weather conditions around ignition time were a temperature of 70 degrees, relative humidity of 23 percent, and winds out of the WSW at 15 knots gusting to 27 knots. A Red Flag Warning was in effect.

Behind the cold front, another surge of strong northwest winds in the evening gusted from 40 to 46 mph. The peak wind gust during the early morning hours was 43 mph at Poplar Bluff. The peak gust in the evening was 44 mph at Poplar Bluff and 46 mph at Cape Girardeau.

An upper-level disturbance and associated cold front pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of March 7, 2022.  The cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass and a line of thunderstorms developed across western Arkansas. The line pushed east into the Mid-South resulting in a few large hail and damaging wind reports.

During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of rain fell in a 6 to 10 hour period with the heaviest rain across the southern half of Audrain County. Numerous roads were flooded including U.S. Business 54, East Liberty Road at Reading Farms, South Morris Street, Lakeview Road at Elmwood Road, Fairground Road and Park Crest Road. Four vehicles were stranded on the flooded roadways. All of the vehicle occupants were moved to safety and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of rain fell in a 6 to 10 hour period causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded across the northern half of the county, including Highway 19 just south of Wellsville, Route 161 between Buell and Route V, Route CC near White Oak Road, and Route E between Kneubuhler Road and Pine Knot Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV moved across southern Missouri during the day on July 2nd. Numerous showers and storms developed as it moved east across the region. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees on the north side of Fredericktown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of October 22nd many wildfires burned across the region, especially across central Missouri where temperatures reached the mid 80s, winds gusted out of the south southwest to 35 mph, and relative humidity was as low as 25 percent. Furthermore, severe drought conditions and back-to-back hard freezes just days before created very dry and cured fuels. The most noteworthy fire on this day occurred in and around the small community of Wooldridge, MO in far eastern Cooper County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 330 pm CDT on October 22nd, a harvesting combine in a crop field started a wildland fire in the small community of Wooldridge, MO (population: 29) in far eastern Cooper County, about 2 miles west of the Missouri River and about 4 miles south of Interstate 70.  ||Weather conditions on the afternoon of 10/22 were quite conducive for fire ignition and rapid fire spread. Relative humidity from the Boonville, MO AWOS (KVER) showed RH values around 25% around fire ignition time (with a temperature of 86 degrees!) with winds out of the SSE around 25 mph and gusting to 35+ mph. Furthermore, antecedent conditions also favored fire ignition and spread. At the time of the fire, Wooldridge was located within an extreme drought (D3) region per the US Drought Monitor. Back to back hard freezes during the overnight/early morning hours of October 18th and 19th (26 and 25 degrees, respectively, per the Boonville COOP observer) had further cured/dried fuels. The previous afternoon had also featured well above normal temperatures, low RHs, and breezy southerly winds. All of these ingredients combined to set the table for a wildland fire. A RAWS site just about 16 miles ESE of Wooldridge near Ashland, MO was reporting 5% fuel moisture around ignition time (3 pm on 10/22). ||The fire spread rapidly to the north following ignition. It destroyed about half the town of Wooldridge, or 23 structures, and then burned northward in the open fields of the Overton Bottoms just west of the Missouri River and just south of Interstate 70. The thick, black smoke produced by the fire shut down Interstate 70 in both directions between Rocheport (Boone County - LSX CWA) and Boonville (Cooper County - EAX CWA) for several hours on the evening of 10/22, with traffic being diverted to alternate routes. Traffic on I-70 was a bit busier than usual for a Saturday evening as the University of Missouri football game had concluded shortly before the smoke shut down the interstate (a substantial percentage of attendees live in the KC metro area). Construction on the new Missouri River I-70 bridge was also halted due to the thick smoke. All told, the fire consumed between 3,000 and 3,500 acres, per estimates from the Cooper County Fire Protection District. 50 agencies/fire departments across the state of Missouri responded to help contain and extinguish the fire. The fire was largely contained by late evening on 10/22, with crews continuing to put out hotspots that redeveloped for several days to follow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the region on the morning of October 12th produced a few strong to marginally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from the size of peas to quarters in Adrian.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wintry precipitation overspread southwestern Missouri during the morning hours of February 23, then the remainder of southern and central Missouri into the afternoon. Much of the precipitation fell as sleet, but some heavier pockets of intense snowfall fell in areas just north of Interstate 44. A second round of sleet and freezing rain showers, with thunder then moved across the area during the morning hours of February 24. Overall, roads became ice covered and hazardous with even a couple of road closures in the far southern part of Missouri due to the hazardous driving conditions. Up to 2 inches of sleet accumulations were reported, along with a glaze of ice from both rounds of precipitation. Numerous schools were closed, along with some county governments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of wintry precipitation fell across the county. The first round began during the late morning hours of February 23, which was mainly sleet and the second round fell during the morning hours of February 24 and was sleet, freezing rain and snow. Thunder was reported with the second round, which produced brief, but heavy precipitation rates. CoCoRaHS station MO-LW-4, 6 miles west northwest of Miller reported 1.3 inches of sleet and snow and a glaze of ice. Roads became covered with numerous schools closed and numerous accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of southeast Missouri received a winter storm, consisting of two periods of sleet and freezing rain. The winter storm area included all but the two Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky. Within the winter storm area, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred during the evening of the 23rd, followed by a more significant round of wintry precipitation on the 24th. Ice accumulations ranged from one-quarter to one-third inch, along with one-half to three-quarters inch of sleet. The precipitation on the 24th was accompanied by frequent thunder and lightning at times. Driving conditions were hazardous. Isolated power outages occurred due to tree damage from freezing rain accretion. Hundreds of customers were without power at the peak of the outages. Over the Kentucky border counties of New Madrid and Mississippi, there was only one round of impactful winter precipitation. This round occurred on the evening of the 23rd, causing travel hazards during the evening commute. The accumulations of sleet and freezing rain in these areas, including New Madrid, were generally below one-quarter inch. Temperatures on the 24th were just warm enough for little or no impact from the precipitation in this area. ||The wintry precipitation was caused by a moist southwest flow aloft, associated with a 500 mb trough over the western Plains. At the same time, surface high pressure advancing from the northern Plains produced a cold northerly surface wind. The cold low levels enabled the precipitation to stay freezing rain and sleet over most of southeast Missouri, but temperatures inched above freezing around New Madrid. The second round of precipitation on the 24th was enhanced by a low pressure center that tracked northeast across Tennessee.

A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2 rounds of wintry precipitation that fell across the county. Rain changed to a mix of sleet and snow during the late evening hours of February 1, then to all snow before ending around midday February 2. There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began during the evening hours of February 2 and continued into the mid morning hours of February 3. CoCoRaHS station MO-MG-7 2.7 miles east of Versailles estimated a total snow and sleet accumulation of 10 inches along with a thin glaze of ice.  Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of southeast Missouri received a winter storm, consisting of two periods of sleet and freezing rain. The winter storm area included all but the two Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky. Within the winter storm area, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred during the evening of the 23rd, followed by a more significant round of wintry precipitation on the 24th. Ice accumulations ranged from one-quarter to one-third inch, along with one-half to three-quarters inch of sleet. The precipitation on the 24th was accompanied by frequent thunder and lightning at times. Driving conditions were hazardous. Isolated power outages occurred due to tree damage from freezing rain accretion. Hundreds of customers were without power at the peak of the outages. Over the Kentucky border counties of New Madrid and Mississippi, there was only one round of impactful winter precipitation. This round occurred on the evening of the 23rd, causing travel hazards during the evening commute. The accumulations of sleet and freezing rain in these areas, including New Madrid, were generally below one-quarter inch. Temperatures on the 24th were just warm enough for little or no impact from the precipitation in this area. ||The wintry precipitation was caused by a moist southwest flow aloft, associated with a 500 mb trough over the western Plains. At the same time, surface high pressure advancing from the northern Plains produced a cold northerly surface wind. The cold low levels enabled the precipitation to stay freezing rain and sleet over most of southeast Missouri, but temperatures inched above freezing around New Madrid. The second round of precipitation on the 24th was enhanced by a low pressure center that tracked northeast across Tennessee.

A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet and snow just after midnight February 2 and ended late in the afternoon. After a brief lull, snow began to fall once again late in the evening of February 2 and continued into the afternoon hours February 3. The Cooperative Observer in Rolla reported a total of 7.4 inches of sleet and snow. A glaze to as much as a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation was reported. Roads quickly became ice and snow covered and slippery. Schools were closed from February 2 to February 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped significant amounts of snow and sleet across southeast Missouri. Some freezing rain also occurred, mainly along the Mississippi River from New Madrid to Charleston. The highest snow amounts were northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Jackson (Cape Girardeau County) line, where most places reported 4 to 5 inches of mainly snow on a layer of sleet. Isolated amounts of 6 to 7 inches were reported in the Ozark foothills of Carter, Wayne, and western Perry Counties. A zone of heavy sleet occurred from Cape Girardeau through Poplar Bluff to Doniphan, where 2 to 3 inches of sleet and snow was common. In the Mississippi River counties south of Sikeston (Scott County), where precipitation was all sleet and freezing rain, one-tenth inch to one-quarter inch of ice was under about three-quarters inch of sleet. Widespread major travel impacts were reported across the region. Roads were covered with an icy mix that resulted in traffic accidents. Isolated power outages were reported in the areas of heavy sleet and freezing rain. A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri on February 2nd. Precipitation changed from rain to a mix of wintry precipitation in the afternoon and evening of the 2nd. Northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Cape Girardeau line, the cold air became deep enough for plenty of snow.

Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms moved across the region. Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches created pockets of road flooding and raised small rivers above flood stage. The heavy rain occurred ahead of a cold front as it entered southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the northwest. Weak waves of low pressure moved northeast along the front. A strong 850 mb jet up to 60 to 70 knots was accompanied by a strong surge of warm moist air through the Mississippi River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few rural roads were flooded in low-lying areas. The Missouri Highway Patrol reported two women safely escaped from a sport utility vehicle that was driven into a flooded area. The incident happened southwest of Poplar Bluff on a rural road about two miles west of Missouri Highway 441.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved rapidly east-northeastward during the early morning hours. A few microbursts accompanied the storms. A low amplitude mid-level shortwave trough over the southern Plains ejected northeast into the Ohio Valley, providing support for the broken line of storms. A surface low tracked northeast across Missouri and Illinois. Within the warm sector of this low pressure system, buoyancy was very weak but adequate for thunderstorms. An intense low level jet over 70 knots translated northeast across Arkansas toward southeast Missouri. The very strong winds near the surface contributed to the microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey revealed a microburst with estimated peak winds near 90 mph struck south central Butler County. The microburst began just north of Allred Lake Natural Area along County Road 2601 and ended along County Road 214 in Coon Island Conservation Area. The maximum path width was about 1000 yards. A semi and an empty grain bin were both flipped onto their sides. Three wooden electrical poles were pushed nearly to the ground. A grain silo was severely dented. A mobile home was shifted off its foundation, and a small portion of its roof decking was removed. Several outbuildings were severely damaged or destroyed. One brick home suffered wind damage to several of its window sills. A handful of trees were uprooted, and several large tree limbs were broken.

Numerous accidents were reported the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2 rounds of wintry precipitation that fell across the county. Rain changed to a mix of freezing rain sleet and snow during the early morning hours of February 2, then changed to all snow before ending around midday. There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began late in the afternoon and continued into the early morning hours of February 3. Generally 3 to  6 inches of snow fell across the county, with the highest amounts in the far southeastern sections of the county. The CoCoRaHS station MO-FSA-217 near Nevada reported a total snow and sleet accumulation of 5.0 inches, while the Cooperative Observer 1 mile west of Nevada reported a total snowfall of 3.5 inches. A glaze of ice was also reported. Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved rapidly east-northeastward during the early morning hours. A few microbursts accompanied the storms. A low amplitude mid-level shortwave trough over the southern Plains ejected northeast into the Ohio Valley, providing support for the broken line of storms. A surface low tracked northeast across Missouri and Illinois. Within the warm sector of this low pressure system, buoyancy was very weak but adequate for thunderstorms. An intense low level jet over 70 knots translated northeast across Arkansas toward southeast Missouri. The very strong winds near the surface contributed to the microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small portion of a large metal building collapsed, with parts of the metal roof blown off. Several houses and other buildings lost some siding and shingles. Large barns suffered roof damage. A few trees were uprooted, and a few large tree limbs were downed. Irrigation pipes were blown around. Peak winds were estimated around 80 mph by a National Weather Service damage survey team.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of southeast Missouri received a winter storm, consisting of two periods of sleet and freezing rain. The winter storm area included all but the two Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky. Within the winter storm area, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred during the evening of the 23rd, followed by a more significant round of wintry precipitation on the 24th. Ice accumulations ranged from one-quarter to one-third inch, along with one-half to three-quarters inch of sleet. The precipitation on the 24th was accompanied by frequent thunder and lightning at times. Driving conditions were hazardous. Isolated power outages occurred due to tree damage from freezing rain accretion. Hundreds of customers were without power at the peak of the outages. Over the Kentucky border counties of New Madrid and Mississippi, there was only one round of impactful winter precipitation. This round occurred on the evening of the 23rd, causing travel hazards during the evening commute. The accumulations of sleet and freezing rain in these areas, including New Madrid, were generally below one-quarter inch. Temperatures on the 24th were just warm enough for little or no impact from the precipitation in this area. ||The wintry precipitation was caused by a moist southwest flow aloft, associated with a 500 mb trough over the western Plains. At the same time, surface high pressure advancing from the northern Plains produced a cold northerly surface wind. The cold low levels enabled the precipitation to stay freezing rain and sleet over most of southeast Missouri, but temperatures inched above freezing around New Madrid. The second round of precipitation on the 24th was enhanced by a low pressure center that tracked northeast across Tennessee.

Wintry precipitation overspread southwestern Missouri during the morning hours of February 23, then the remainder of southern and central Missouri into the afternoon. Much of the precipitation fell as sleet, but some heavier pockets of intense snowfall fell in areas just north of Interstate 44. A second round of sleet and freezing rain showers, with thunder then moved across the area during the morning hours of February 24. Overall, roads became ice covered and hazardous with even a couple of road closures in the far southern part of Missouri due to the hazardous driving conditions. Up to 2 inches of sleet accumulations were reported, along with a glaze of ice from both rounds of precipitation. Numerous schools were closed, along with some county governments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of wintry precipitation fell across the county. The first round began during the morning hours of February 23, which was mainly snow and a little sleet and the second round fell during the morning hours of February 24 and was sleet, freezing rain and snow. Thunder was reported with the second round, which produced brief, but heavy precipitation rates. The Cooperative Observer 7 miles north of Lamar reported 2.5 inches of sleet and snow accumulation, with most it falling as snow. Roads became covered with numerous schools closed and numerous accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped significant amounts of snow and sleet across southeast Missouri. Some freezing rain also occurred, mainly along the Mississippi River from New Madrid to Charleston. The highest snow amounts were northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Jackson (Cape Girardeau County) line, where most places reported 4 to 5 inches of mainly snow on a layer of sleet. Isolated amounts of 6 to 7 inches were reported in the Ozark foothills of Carter, Wayne, and western Perry Counties. A zone of heavy sleet occurred from Cape Girardeau through Poplar Bluff to Doniphan, where 2 to 3 inches of sleet and snow was common. Widespread major travel impacts were reported across the region. Roads were covered with an icy mix that resulted in traffic accidents. Isolated power outages were reported in the areas of heavy sleet and freezing rain. A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri on February 2nd. Precipitation changed from rain to a mix of wintry precipitation in the afternoon and evening of the 2nd. Northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Cape Girardeau line, the cold air became deep enough for plenty of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought rain initially to the area, with the rain changing over to freezing rain, sleet and snow during the early morning hours of February 17. The combination of the freezing rain and sleet/snow caused roads to become like a block of ice according to the Missouri Department of Transportation. Little if any snow or mixed precipitation fell to the south of Interstate 44.

The Cooperative Observer 1 mile northeast of Bolivar reported 0.4 inches of sleet and snow. Roads were snow and ice covered and hazardous, especially across the northern sections of the county, where a report of 1.0 inches of sleet fell along the St. Clair county line 2 miles northwest of Humansville.  There were numerous side-offs and schools were closed for the day.

Rain changed to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow during the early morning hours of February 2, then changed to all snow before ending around midday. This first round brought around a tenth of an inch of ice along with 1 to 3 inches of snowfall. There was a lull in the action before a second round of snow began during the evening hours of February 2 and continued into the afternoon hours of February 3. This round brought 3 to 5 inches of snow to the county. The third round of snow that began during the evening hours of February 3 and continued into the early morning hours of February 4, affected mainly the southern part of the county with another 1 inch of snowfall. The Cooperative Observer in Buffalo reported a storm total of 6.0 inches of snowfall. Schools were closed across the county through the entire period. Numerous accidents were reported beginning the morning of February 2 as roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wintry precipitation overspread southwestern Missouri during the morning hours of February 23, then the remainder of southern and central Missouri into the afternoon. Much of the precipitation fell as sleet, but some heavier pockets of intense snowfall fell in areas just north of Interstate 44. A second round of sleet and freezing rain showers, with thunder then moved across the area during the morning hours of February 24. Overall, roads became ice covered and hazardous with even a couple of road closures in the far southern part of Missouri due to the hazardous driving conditions. Up to 2 inches of sleet accumulations were reported, along with a glaze of ice from both rounds of precipitation. Numerous schools were closed, along with some county governments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of wintry precipitation fell across the county. The first round began during the early afternoon of February 23, which was mainly snow and a little sleet and the second round fell during the morning hours of February 24 and was sleet, freezing rain and snow. Thunder was reported with the second round, which produced brief, but heavy precipitation rates. The Cooperative Observer 1 mile northwest of Iberia measured 1.3 inches of total of sleet and snow, with most of it snow. Roads became covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and moist airmass pushed into the Mid-South by early morning on February 22, 2022.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front producing damaging winds across eastern Arkansas and northwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house on Highway J. A motorist driving on Highway J had a tree fall on their car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped significant amounts of snow and sleet across southeast Missouri. Some freezing rain also occurred, mainly along the Mississippi River from New Madrid to Charleston. The highest snow amounts were northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Jackson (Cape Girardeau County) line, where most places reported 4 to 5 inches of mainly snow on a layer of sleet. Isolated amounts of 6 to 7 inches were reported in the Ozark foothills of Carter, Wayne, and western Perry Counties. A zone of heavy sleet occurred from Cape Girardeau through Poplar Bluff to Doniphan, where 2 to 3 inches of sleet and snow was common. In the Mississippi River counties south of Sikeston (Scott County), where precipitation was all sleet and freezing rain, one-tenth inch to one-quarter inch of ice was under about three-quarters inch of sleet. Widespread major travel impacts were reported across the region. Roads were covered with an icy mix that resulted in traffic accidents. Isolated power outages were reported in the areas of heavy sleet and freezing rain. A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri on February 2nd. Precipitation changed from rain to a mix of wintry precipitation in the afternoon and evening of the 2nd. Northwest of a Poplar Bluff to Cape Girardeau line, the cold air became deep enough for plenty of snow.

A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2 rounds of wintry precipitation that fell across the county. Rain changed to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow during the early morning hours of February 2, then changed to all snow before ending around midday. There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began late in the afternoon and continued into the early morning hours of February 3.  The Cooperative observer 7 miles north of Lamar measured a total snow and sleet accumulation of 6.5 inches, along with a glaze of ice. Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms moved across the region. Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches created pockets of road flooding and raised small rivers above flood stage. The heavy rain occurred ahead of a cold front as it entered southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the northwest. Weak waves of low pressure moved northeast along the front. A strong 850 mb jet up to 60 to 70 knots was accompanied by a strong surge of warm moist air through the Mississippi River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the St. Francis River. Low-lying woods, fields, and some campground land was under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of southeast Missouri received a winter storm, consisting of two periods of sleet and freezing rain. The winter storm area included all but the two Mississippi River counties bordering Kentucky. Within the winter storm area, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred during the evening of the 23rd, followed by a more significant round of wintry precipitation on the 24th. Ice accumulations ranged from one-quarter to one-third inch, along with one-half to three-quarters inch of sleet. The precipitation on the 24th was accompanied by frequent thunder and lightning at times. Driving conditions were hazardous. Isolated power outages occurred due to tree damage from freezing rain accretion. Hundreds of customers were without power at the peak of the outages. Over the Kentucky border counties of New Madrid and Mississippi, there was only one round of impactful winter precipitation. This round occurred on the evening of the 23rd, causing travel hazards during the evening commute. The accumulations of sleet and freezing rain in these areas, including New Madrid, were generally below one-quarter inch. Temperatures on the 24th were just warm enough for little or no impact from the precipitation in this area. ||The wintry precipitation was caused by a moist southwest flow aloft, associated with a 500 mb trough over the western Plains. At the same time, surface high pressure advancing from the northern Plains produced a cold northerly surface wind. The cold low levels enabled the precipitation to stay freezing rain and sleet over most of southeast Missouri, but temperatures inched above freezing around New Madrid. The second round of precipitation on the 24th was enhanced by a low pressure center that tracked northeast across Tennessee.

A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 3 rounds of wintry precipitation that fell across the county. Rain changed to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow during the early morning hours of February 2, then changed to all snow before ending around midday. There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began early that evening and continued into the day February 3. The ASOS in Joplin reported 0.11 inches of flat ice accumulation with the initial round, with other reports of 1 to 2 inches of sleet and snow.  The third round of snow began the evening of February 3 and ending during the early morning hours of February 4. Additional snow amounts with the second and third rounds resulted in total snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches, with the higher amounts across the eastern sections of the county. The CoCoRaHS station 4.5 miles east of Carthage reported a total sleet and snow accumulation of 4.5 inches. Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3 and 4th. A Carthage special Road District snow plow slid off the road and overturned the morning of February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wintry precipitation overspread southwestern Missouri during the morning hours of February 23, then the remainder of southern and central Missouri into the afternoon. Much of the precipitation fell as sleet, but some heavier pockets of intense snowfall fell in areas just north of Interstate 44. A second round of sleet and freezing rain showers, with thunder then moved across the area during the morning hours of February 24. Overall, roads became ice covered and hazardous with even a couple of road closures in the far southern part of Missouri due to the hazardous driving conditions. Up to 2 inches of sleet accumulations were reported, along with a glaze of ice from both rounds of precipitation. Numerous schools were closed, along with some county governments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of wintry precipitation fell across the county. The first round began during the late morning hours of February 23, which was mainly sleet and the second round fell during the morning hours of February 24 and was sleet, freezing rain and snow. Thunder was reported with the second round, which produced brief, but heavy precipitation rates.  CoCoRaHS station MO-JS-30, 4 miles east of Carthage reported 0.6 inches of sleet and 0.3 inches of snowfall. Meanwhile the ASOS at the Joplin Regional Airport reported near .10 of an inch if ice accumulation. Roads became covered with numerous schools closed and numerous accidents were reported.

A cold front, that was draped northeast-southwest across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska/Kansas, moved south and entered the county warning area during the morning of February 1st.  Broad moist isentropic ascent and overrunning precipitation developed across the entire county warning area gradually through the late morning into the afternoon of February 1st, with the baroclinic zone initially serving as the focus for rain. This first round of precipitation stayed nearly all rain, but snow did begin to mix with rain in northern Missouri and west-central Illinois during the afternoon hours of February 1st as colder air advanced south behind the front.  The cold air became more established in the northwest half of the CWA with wet snow and a snowfall rate approaching 1/hr across parts of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois north of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of February 2nd.  The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of precipitation with this system. The first round was rain changing to a mix of sleet and snow, then another round of heavy snow. Up to half an inch of sleet was reported in parts of the county during the morning hours of February 2nd. With the second round of precipitation, storm total snowfall ranged from 4 to 8.5 inches.

There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began during the evening hours of February 2 and continued into the mid morning hours of February 3. The Cooperative Observer 3 miles northwest of Cole Camp reported a snow and sleet total of 4.5 inches along with a thin glaze of ice.  Other estimates of 4 to 6 inches of sleet and snow were reported around the county. Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southern Missouri. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet freezing and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, with another round of snow moving in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through much of the day February 3. A 3rd round of snow began in the evening of the 3rd and lasted into the early morning hours of February 4 dropping another 1 to 3 inches of snow in some counties across far southern Missouri. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round, with storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranging from 3 to 10 inches, with the lower accumulations over south central Missouri and along the Kansas border. Missouri Highway Patrol Troop D that covers southwest Missouri reported  229 calls, 147 stranded motorists and 51 crashes between from midnight and 8 p.m. on February 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to freezing rain during the early morning hours of February 2 before changing to snow shortly before ending. Widespread reported of a glaze to a tenth of an inch of ice was reported around the county. Roads became ice covered and hazardous. Schools were closed for the day. After a brief lull, 2 more rounds of snow impacted the county from the evening of February 2 to the early morning hours of February 4. Widespread snowfall reported of 6 to 8 inches were reported around the county with the last 2 rounds of snow.  Roads became snow covered and slippery with a number of accidents being reported. All schools were closed from February 2 through February 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impactful winter storm moved through the Midwest on New Year's Day, which brought snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the region. Serious travel impacts occurred across portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Most of the area in the Winter Storm Warning only got between 1 and 4 inches of snow. However, there were several reports of 5 to 7 inches of snow over Knox County, a mix of up to 5 inches of snow and half an inch of sleet in Lewis County, and up to an inch of sleet in Quincy, IL (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Knox County on New Year's Day. The co-op observer in Edina reported a storm total of 6.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward through the Mid-South on Tuesday, February 22, 2022, eventually stalling across central Mississippi by Wednesday, February 23, 2022. Cold air gradually deepened across northern sections of the Mid-South. A potent upper-level trough approached the region Wednesday afternoon resulting in a burst of sleet across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel which quickly impacted travel.  Moisture continued to stream across the region Wednesday night into Thursday morning as cold air seeped south toward the I-40 corridor in Arkansas and across northwest Tennessee. This resulted in a corridor of heavy icing across much of east Arkansas and northwest Tennessee. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread power outages caused by tree and powerline damage. Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one inch of sleet fell resulting in widespread travel disruption. Around one-tenth of an inch of ice resulted in some tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of sleet resulted in widespread travel disruption.
