EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest airmass since December of 1989 descended upon the Mid-South right before Christmas. Gusty north winds combined with very cold temperatures produced wind chills as low as 25 degrees below zero across the Missouri Bootheel from late Thursday, December 22nd into Friday, December 23rd. Temperatures dropped to between 0 and 5 degrees below zero during the morning of Friday, December 23rd. Numerous frozen pipes occurred in residential houses and businesses across the region. Wind chills remained below zero into the morning hours of December 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values fell to around 25 degrees below zero and low temperatures reached 3 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, that was draped northeast-southwest across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska/Kansas, moved south and entered the county warning area during the morning of February 1st.  Broad moist isentropic ascent and overrunning precipitation developed across the entire county warning area gradually through the late morning into the afternoon of February 1st, with the baroclinic zone initially serving as the focus for rain. This first round of precipitation stayed nearly all rain, but snow did begin to mix with rain in northern Missouri and west-central Illinois during the afternoon hours of February 1st as colder air advanced south behind the front.  The cold air became more established in the northwest half of the CWA with wet snow and a snowfall rate approaching 1/hr across parts of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois north of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of February 2nd.  The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of snow with this system. Snowfall ranged from 8 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon of Thursday, May 19, 2022, a complex of thunderstorms expanded and intensified over sections of south-central Missouri. Thunderstorms quickly became strong to severe as the complex tracked east-northeast, impacting east-central Missouri, southeast Missouri, west-central Illinois and southwest Illinois. Numerous thunderstorms embedded within the broad complex exhibited signs of rotation, resulting in nine confirmed tornadoes. Of the nine tornadoes, six tornadoes occurred in Missouri and three occurred in Illinois. In Missouri, five of the six tornadoes were rated EF-0 and one tornado was rated EF-1. In Illinois, two tornadoes were rated EF-0, while the third was rated EF-1. In addition to the nine tornadoes, several locations were impacted by straight-line wind damage. The greatest impact from straight-line winds extended west to east through sections of southeast Missouri into southwest Illinois. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, that was draped northeast-southwest across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska/Kansas, moved south and entered the county warning area during the morning of February 1st.  Broad moist isentropic ascent and overrunning precipitation developed across the entire county warning area gradually through the late morning into the afternoon of February 1st, with the baroclinic zone initially serving as the focus for rain. This first round of precipitation stayed nearly all rain, but snow did begin to mix with rain in northern Missouri and west-central Illinois during the afternoon hours of February 1st as colder air advanced south behind the front.  The cold air became more established in the northwest half of the CWA with wet snow and a snowfall rate approaching 1/hr across parts of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois north of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of February 2nd.  The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of snow fell across Shelby County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms accompanied a cold front as it moved southeast across southeast Missouri. The first round brought a wide swath of at least 6-10 inches of snow (with some sleet mixed in) across central/east-central Missouri and west-central Illinois. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across Audrain County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon of Thursday, May 19, 2022, a complex of thunderstorms expanded and intensified over sections of south-central Missouri. Thunderstorms quickly became strong to severe as the complex tracked east-northeast, impacting east-central Missouri, southeast Missouri, west-central Illinois and southwest Illinois. Numerous thunderstorms embedded within the broad complex exhibited signs of rotation, resulting in nine confirmed tornadoes. Of the nine tornadoes, six tornadoes occurred in Missouri and three occurred in Illinois. In Missouri, five of the six tornadoes were rated EF-0 and one tornado was rated EF-1. In Illinois, two tornadoes were rated EF-0, while the third was rated EF-1. In addition to the nine tornadoes, several locations were impacted by straight-line wind damage. The greatest impact from straight-line winds extended west to east through sections of southeast Missouri into southwest Illinois. The most extensive straight-line wind damage was in a swath from Desloge, MO through Chester, IL. Between the tornadoes and straight-line winds, much of the damage was similar in nature, taking out several trees, damaging outbuildings, machine sheds, and a few grain silos. Some residential homes also suffered damage, largely due to fallen trees, large limbs and/or flying debris. No fatalities nor injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs around the intersection of La Bonne Parkway and La Marite Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest airmass since December of 1989 descended upon the Mid-South right before Christmas. Gusty north winds combined with very cold temperatures produced wind chills as low as 25 degrees below zero across the Missouri Bootheel from late Thursday, December 22nd into Friday, December 23rd. Temperatures dropped to between 0 and 5 degrees below zero during the morning of Friday, December 23rd. Numerous frozen pipes occurred in residential houses and businesses across the region. Wind chills remained below zero into the morning hours of December 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values fell to around 25 degrees below zero and low temperatures reached 4 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, that was draped northeast-southwest across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska/Kansas, moved south and entered the county warning area during the morning of February 1st.  Broad moist isentropic ascent and overrunning precipitation developed across the entire county warning area gradually through the late morning into the afternoon of February 1st, with the baroclinic zone initially serving as the focus for rain. This first round of precipitation stayed nearly all rain, but snow did begin to mix with rain in northern Missouri and west-central Illinois during the afternoon hours of February 1st as colder air advanced south behind the front. A second round of heavy snow fell along and south of the I-70 corridor with snowfall rates in excess of 0.5 per hour and occasionally brief periods of 1.5 per hour on Thursday, February 3rd.  An additional 6-8 of snow fell in this area during the morning/afternoon hours of February 3rd with lighter snowfall over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to twelve inches of snow fell across Marion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few supercell thunderstorms formed over southeast Missouri during the warmth of the afternoon. The storms gradually intensified immediately ahead of a cold front across southeast Missouri, where lower 60's dewpoints were in place. Pockets of diurnal heating resulted in moderate instability, with mixed layer cape values from 1500 to 2000. A moderately strong low level southerly wind flow was adequate for a brief tornado or two.  With deep-layer winds oriented perpendicular to the initiating cold front boundary, conditions were favorable for supercells capable of locally damaging winds, isolated large hail, and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs from two to three inches in diameter were down. This supercell thunderstorm also produced large hail from Van Buren east-northeast along Highway 60.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported large hail from quarter size to ping-pong ball size. The large hail occurred in a west to east swath around the Perryville airport. The large hail accompanied a supercell thunderstorm across northern Perry County. Damaging winds and a brief tornado occurred just slightly south of the hail swath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto a house. This report was received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of storms with heavy rainfall moved into the region ahead of a cold front from the early morning through the early afternoon hours. The most notable flooding occurred at Roaring River State Park, where over three inches of rain caused the Roaring River to rise over 6 feet within a two-hour period. Evacuations were conducted with over 30 people moved from the campground near the river. Other reports of flooding were received along and south of interstate 44 where numerous low water crossings flooded. Most of the flood reports were received from areas where over two inches of rain fell during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Highway 76 was closed to traffic east of MO-112 due to flooding.

The storms gradually intensified immediately ahead of a cold front across southeast Missouri, where lower 60's dewpoints were in place. Pockets of diurnal heating resulted in moderate instability, with mixed layer cape values from 1500 to 2000. A moderately strong low level southerly wind flow was adequate for a brief tornado or two.  With deep-layer winds oriented perpendicular to the initiating cold front boundary, conditions were favorable for supercells capable of locally damaging winds, isolated large hail, and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An small area of damaging straight-line winds occurred on the west side of Interstate 55 on County Road 806. The wind damage area was about 0.4 mile wide and included many large downed trees and tree branches. There was also some shingle damage and a roof torn off a barn. A small EF-0 tornado was embedded within this damage area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few supercell thunderstorms formed over southeast Missouri during the warmth of the afternoon. The storms gradually intensified immediately ahead of a cold front across southeast Missouri, where lower 60's dewpoints were in place. A moderately strong low level southerly wind flow was adequate for a brief tornado or two.  With deep-layer winds oriented perpendicular to the initiating cold front boundary, conditions were favorable for supercells capable of locally damaging winds, isolated large hail, and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down on County Road 806 within a larger area of straight-line winds. The evidence of the weak tornado was based on the orientation of the damage. This included uprooted trees oriented to the southeast, along with lofted tin from a nearby damaged machine shed and a large two-by-four impaled into the ground. The tornado also destroyed a 30-by-50 foot machine shed near some other tree damage in the vicinity. The home next to this machine shed sustained damage to its soffit and gutters. The siding was damaged by debris. Power lines were down. A trained spotter reported seeing a funnel cloud. Peak winds were estimated near 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of storms with heavy rainfall moved into the region ahead of a cold front from the early morning through the early afternoon hours. The most notable flooding occurred at Roaring River State Park, where over three inches of rain caused the Roaring River to rise over 6 feet within a two-hour period. Evacuations were conducted with over 30 people moved from the campground near the river. Other reports of flooding were received along and south of interstate 44 where numerous low water crossings flooded. Most of the flood reports were received from areas where over two inches of rain fell during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway O at Tory Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region from the late evening of the 20th to the early evening of the 21st as low pressure deepened over the Central Plains, causing a warm front to lift northward across the region. Storms trained over extreme southwest Missouri during the early morning of the 21st and led to flooding over McDonald, Newton and Barry counties. Additional storms that developed in the afternoon produced a swath of golf ball to softball size hail from Fort Scott, Kansas to Bolivar, Missouri. Rainfall accumulations for the 24-hour period ending at 7 PM on the 21st showed localized accumulations greater than five inches along the Newton/McDonald County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed over parts of southeast Missouri, mainly north of a line from Poplar Bluff to Cape Girardeau. The lowest temperature reported was 27 degrees at Clearwater Dam in northwest Wayne County. Other low temperatures included 29 degrees at Van Buren in Carter County and 32 degrees at Perryville. The freeze was late enough in the growing season to damage any unprotected strawberry crops, as well as a wide variety of other fruit crops and flowering ornamental shrubs and trees. A surface high pressure ridge was centered from Iowa across eastern Missouri, providing light winds and clear skies favorable for cold early morning temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Marshall Airport reported a 63 mph wind gust. There was also some roof damage at the terminal from these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 10 inches of rain fell in a period of 8 hours causing catastrophic flooding in portions of St. Louis County. Numerous roads were flooded. There were multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. One fatality was reported in Hazelwood where a 39 year old man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck. His body was found about half a mile down stream from the semi truck. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Several hundred homes and businesses sustained minor to major flood damage. A large number of unoccupied vehicles were damaged/destroyed due to the flooding as well.  Dollar amount damage so far is estimated around $50 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved across the region from the late evening of the 20th to the early evening of the 21st as low pressure deepened over the Central Plains, causing a warm front to lift northward across the region. Storms trained over extreme southwest Missouri during the early morning of the 21st and led to flooding over McDonald, Newton and Barry counties. Additional storms that developed in the afternoon produced a swath of golf ball to softball size hail from Fort Scott, Kansas to Bolivar, Missouri. Rainfall accumulations for the 24-hour period ending at 7 PM on the 21st showed localized accumulations greater than five inches along the Newton/McDonald County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused a road to be washed out south of the intersection of Shady Lane and Highway 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter in the area reported 60 mph wind and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of storms with heavy rainfall moved into the region ahead of a cold front from the early morning through the early afternoon hours. The most notable flooding occurred at Roaring River State Park, where over three inches of rain caused the Roaring River to rise over 6 feet within a two-hour period. Evacuations were conducted with over 30 people moved from the campground near the river. Other reports of flooding were received along and south of interstate 44 where numerous low water crossings flooded. Most of the flood reports were received from areas where over two inches of rain fell during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple people camping on a gravel bar near Farm Road 2265 were rescued as Roaring River rose rapidly due to heavy rainfall. All individuals were rescued safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A detached garage was pushed off its foundation in Rushville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Texas County by the middle of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed and produced hail to ping pong balls in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was reported in a mainly rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front from the storm a system on May 2-3 moved northward back into the Ozarks as a warm front as a strong upper level low over southern Colorado moved eastward into the southern Plains. This led to another several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall that occurred from the late morning of the 4th to the early morning of the 6th, when the surface low exited the area. Widespread accumulations of two to five inches occurred, with isolated accumulations over six inches over parts of Jasper and Dade Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding indicated that not only were low water crossings flooding, but major highways as well. Evacuations were conducted at both Roaring River State Park in Barry County and at Bennett Spring State Park on the Dallas/Laclede County Border. Several people were stranded in cabins and RVs at Bennett Spring during the peak of the flood. There were 11 swift water rescues reported in southwest Missouri. Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway U at Silver Lake Branch was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal storm complex developed over southwest Kansas and northwest Oklahoma during the evening of the 18th moved eastward and reached the western edge of the Ozarks during the early morning of the 19th. The storm complex developed into a mesoscale convective vorticity center (MCV) and tracked northeastward across the region through the late morning and afternoon. The airmass on the eastern side of the MCV was unstable enough for severe storms to develop during the afternoon. The storms produced two EF-1 tornadoes in Phelps County south of I-44 near the towns of Beulah and Lecoma. Localized flash flooding was also observed near I-44 between Marshfield and Rolla. The storms also produce areas of widespread wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds damaged a portion of the school gymnasium wall in Success.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front triggered showers and thunderstorms across the region from the late evening of the 20th to the late afternoon of the 21st. A few of the stronger storms produced wind gusts over 60 mph and hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter. There were also several reports of flooding along the Interstate 44 corridor. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one half inch and one inch, with isolated accumulations over 2.5 inches near Fort Leonard Wood and Rolla.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms across Arkansas during the morning hours of April 13, 2022. Storms eventually organized into a line that swept across the Mid-South. A strong low-level jet, large amounts of wind shear, and moderate amounts of instability supported severe thunderstorms across the Mid-South. Large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes were the result. Locally heavy rain also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust of 60 mph by the Missouri Mesonet Station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began just south of County Road West County Road 70 in the Garson area causing damage to hardwood trees. As the tornado moved northeast across agricultural land, several center pivot irrigation systems were rolled. The tornado crossed into Dunklin County near County Road 645, damaging a mobile home unit and additional center pivot irrigation systems along Missouri Route K and County Road 626. An outbuilding was damaged along County Road 655 with the tornado lifting as it moved into Hornersville. Peak winds were estimated at 85 mph. Thanks to NSSL for assistance with this damage survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions continued severe (D2) drought conditions over the Missouri Bootheel through the month of November. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front from the storm a system on May 2-3 moved northward back into the Ozarks as a warm front as a strong upper level low over southern Colorado moved eastward into the southern Plains. This led to another several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall that occurred from the late morning of the 4th to the early morning of the 6th, when the surface low exited the area. Widespread accumulations of two to five inches occurred, with isolated accumulations over six inches over parts of Jasper and Dade Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding indicated that not only were low water crossings flooding, but major highways as well. Evacuations were conducted at both Roaring River State Park in Barry County and at Bennett Spring State Park on the Dallas/Laclede County Border. Several people were stranded in cabins and RVs at Bennett Spring during the peak of the flood. There were 11 swift water rescues reported in southwest Missouri. Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway CC at Indian Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of October. By the last week of October, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 84 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 23 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 6 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across the much of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of October. The US Drought Monitor indicated the southeastern two thirds of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder ranging from Moderate (D1) Drought to Abnormally (D0) Dry in the far northeast. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary losses will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

Evacuations were conducted at both Roaring River State Park in Barry County and at Bennett Spring State Park on the Dallas/Laclede County Border. Several people were stranded in cabins and RVs at Bennett Spring during the peak of the flood. There were 11 swift water rescues reported in southwest Missouri. Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 14 was closed at a low water area due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Laclede County by the end of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms with heavy rainfall impacted the region during the afternoon and evening as an unstable airmass and upper level disturbances interacted with a stalled outflow boundary. Several locations along and east of a line from Lake of the Ozarks to Marshfield to Branson reported hail of quarter to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front from the storm a system on May 2-3 moved northward back into the Ozarks as a warm front as a strong upper level low over southern Colorado moved eastward into the southern Plains. This led to another several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall that occurred from the late morning of the 4th to the early morning of the 6th, when the surface low exited the area. Widespread accumulations of two to five inches occurred, with isolated accumulations over six inches over parts of Jasper and Dade Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding indicated that not only were low water crossings flooding, but major highways as well. Evacuations were conducted at both Roaring River State Park in Barry County and at Bennett Spring State Park on the Dallas/Laclede County Border. Several people were stranded in cabins and RVs at Bennett Spring during the peak of the flood. There were 11 swift water rescues reported in southwest Missouri. Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue was performed on Farm Road 156 at a low water area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms with heavy rainfall impacted the region during the afternoon and evening as an unstable airmass and upper level disturbances interacted with a stalled outflow boundary. Several locations along and east of a line from Lake of the Ozarks to Marshfield to Branson reported hail of quarter to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved southeastward from northwest Missouri into the Ozarks during the morning and early afternoon of the 15th. The strongest storms within this storm complex produced wind gusts over 60 mph and caused damage to trees, powerlines and roofs mainly over southwest Missouri from Mount Vernon to Table Rock Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large oak trees were uprooted along Farm Road 2085. Winds were estimated at up to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A leftover outflow boundary sparked a few thunderstorms across the Missouri Bootheel during the early evening hours on May 14, 2022 resulting in large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moved into the Mid-South. Thunderstorms developed along the Mississippi River and into West Tennessee. Damaging wind, large hail, and a few landspouts were the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout occurred two miles south-southeast of Pascola near the intersection of Highway 412 and County Road 267. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across St. Clair County by the end of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple people in Kennett reported hail estimated at one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeastward across the region during the morning of the 18th. The complex weakened during the late morning with the loss of the low level jet and left an outflow boundary that focused storm development later that afternoon near the Arkansas border. Hail to golf balls in size and damaging winds were produced by the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in Kimberling City. Minor damage to vehicles occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal storm complex developed over southwest Kansas and northwest Oklahoma during the evening of the 18th moved eastward and reached the western edge of the Ozarks during the early morning of the 19th. The storm complex developed into a mesoscale convective vorticity center (MCV) and tracked northeastward across the region through the late morning and afternoon. The airmass on the eastern side of the MCV was unstable enough for severe storms to develop during the afternoon. The storms produced two EF-1 tornadoes in Phelps County south of I-44 near the towns of Beulah and Lecoma. Localized flash flooding was also observed near I-44 between Marshfield and Rolla. The storms also produce areas of widespread wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail was reported at Highway Y and Cowskin Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell in Kennett.

Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River was reported flowing well out of its banks at Highway B.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal storm complex developed over southwest Kansas and northwest Oklahoma during the evening of the 18th moved eastward and reached the western edge of the Ozarks during the early morning of the 19th. The storm complex developed into a mesoscale convective vorticity center (MCV) and tracked northeastward across the region through the late morning and afternoon. The airmass on the eastern side of the MCV was unstable enough for severe storms to develop during the afternoon. The storms produced two EF-1 tornadoes in Phelps County south of I-44 near the towns of Beulah and Lecoma. Localized flash flooding was also observed near I-44 between Marshfield and Rolla. The storms also produce areas of widespread wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Beaver Creek was flooding the area near Martin Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon of May 17 a complex of severe thunderstorms moved through northern Missouri, bringing some minor wind damage and marginally severe hail. Later in the evening another convective complex moved into the the southern KC metro, causing some strong winds after sunset.

A shortwave moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms across Arkansas during the morning hours of April 13, 2022. Storms eventually organized into a line that swept across the Mid-South. A strong low-level jet, large amounts of wind shear, and moderate amounts of instability supported severe thunderstorms across the Mid-South. Large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes were the result. Locally heavy rain also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to roof of new building at Main Street and Highway 412.

Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway AA along the Pomme De Terre River was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front from the storm a system on May 2-3 moved northward back into the Ozarks as a warm front as a strong upper level low over southern Colorado moved eastward into the southern Plains. This led to another several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall that occurred from the late morning of the 4th to the early morning of the 6th, when the surface low exited the area. Widespread accumulations of two to five inches occurred, with isolated accumulations over six inches over parts of Jasper and Dade Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding indicated that not only were low water crossings flooding, but major highways as well. Evacuations were conducted at both Roaring River State Park in Barry County and at Bennett Spring State Park on the Dallas/Laclede County Border. Several people were stranded in cabins and RVs at Bennett Spring during the peak of the flood. There were 11 swift water rescues reported in southwest Missouri. Unfortunately, there was one fatality in Dade County when a 65-year old male drove into a flooded low water crossing and was swept downstream after exiting his vehicle and attempting to swim to shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Main street at Coon Creek was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal storm complex developed over southwest Kansas and northwest Oklahoma during the evening of the 18th moved eastward and reached the western edge of the Ozarks during the early morning of the 19th. The storm complex developed into a mesoscale convective vorticity center (MCV) and tracked northeastward across the region through the late morning and afternoon. The airmass on the eastern side of the MCV was unstable enough for severe storms to develop during the afternoon. The storms produced two EF-1 tornadoes in Phelps County south of I-44 near the towns of Beulah and Lecoma. Localized flash flooding was also observed near I-44 between Marshfield and Rolla. The storms also produce areas of widespread wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 90 mph uprooted numerous trees along its 1 mile long path. A few trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Polk County by the end of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.

Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms with heavy rainfall impacted the region during the afternoon and evening as an unstable airmass and upper level disturbances interacted with a stalled outflow boundary. Several locations along and east of a line from Lake of the Ozarks to Marshfield to Branson reported hail of quarter to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of February 1 light to moderate snow formed across western and central Missouri. The heavier band of snow set up more in central Missouri, where roughly 6 to 12 inches of snow fell. Within that 6-12 inch band the area around Marshall and Moberly, Missouri and areas to the east received the higher amounts within that range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 8 to 12 inches of snow fell overnight on the 1st, going into the early morning hours on the 2nd of February. More snow moved in a few hours later, but did not appear to contribute significantly to the totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 9 inches of rain fell in a period of 8 hours causing catastrophic flooding in portions of the city of St. Louis. Numerous roads were flooded. There were multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the City of St. Louis. One fatality was at the intersection of Skinker Boulevard and Enright Avenue, just north of the Delmar Loop, where a 60 year old man drowned after driving into the flooded roadway. Firefighters helped or rescued around 70 residents. Property damage was very significant in some hard-hit areas, including the southwest part of the city where 14 to 15 homes experienced significant flooding. A large number of unoccupied vehicles were damaged/destroyed due to the flooding as well. The Metro Link station at Forest Park Parkway and DeBaliviere Avenue was flooded causing $35 million in damage to a two car train, tracks, towers, vehicles and equipment at the station. Dollar amount damage so far is estimated around $85 million.

Hail to golf balls in size and damaging winds were produced by the strongest storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon of Thursday, May 19, 2022, a complex of thunderstorms expanded and intensified over sections of south-central Missouri. Thunderstorms quickly became strong to severe as the complex tracked east-northeast, impacting east-central Missouri, southeast Missouri, west-central Illinois and southwest Illinois. Numerous thunderstorms embedded within the broad complex exhibited signs of rotation, resulting in nine confirmed tornadoes. Of the nine tornadoes, six tornadoes occurred in Missouri and three occurred in Illinois. In Missouri, five of the six tornadoes were rated EF-0 and one tornado was rated EF-1. In Illinois, two tornadoes were rated EF-0, while the third was rated EF-1. In addition to the nine tornadoes, several locations were impacted by straight-line wind damage. The greatest impact from straight-line winds extended west to east through sections of southeast Missouri into southwest Illinois. The most extensive straight-line wind damage was in a swath from Desloge, MO through Chester, IL. Between the tornadoes and straight-line winds, much of the damage was similar in nature, taking out several trees, damaging outbuildings, machine sheds, and a few grain silos. Some residential homes also suffered damage, largely due to fallen trees, large limbs and/or flying debris. No fatalities nor injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down around 4:16 PM along Anaconda Road approximately 2 miles southwest of St. Clair, MO in Franklin County. The tornado produced tree damage along its path as it headed north-northwest. It dissipated around 4:18 PM just south of Route WW. Overall, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 2.53 miles and a max path width of 50 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado formed off of East Jefferson Avenue in Kirkwood and began moving north/northeastward through the Glendale neighborhood. The tornado continued northward, crossing Manchester Road near Bennett Avenue while producing more tree damage. The tornado crossed through Old Warson Country Club and then dissipated as it crossed Litzsinger Road. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of the month, two storm systems moved out of the southern Plains. Scattered showers and thunderstorms brought around an inch of rain on average, while a few locations received up to 2.5 inches. Rises on the Mississippi River were sufficient for some minor flooding in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Mississippi River, inundating some low-lying fields. Riverfront access was limited due to flooding of access roads and boat ramps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved across the Mid-South. This feature interacted with a remnant outflow boundary resulting in thunderstorm development. Damaging wind, large hail, and heavy rain were reported across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell four miles south of Malden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Greene County by the end of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex produced isolated minor street flooding, followed by minor flooding of the St. Francis River. The thunderstorms were triggered by an early morning mesoscale convective vortex that tracked northeast from northwest Arkansas. A thunderstorm line propagated eastward, producing a short-duration heavy rain event. Over a wider region, enhanced low-level winds ahead of the convective vortex helped support a broad area of rain. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches over a fairly short period of time was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the St. Francis River. Some minor flooding of low-lying fields and woodlands occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined that straight line winds estimated at 60 mph uprooted a few trees and broke some large limbs at a couple of homes along Farm Road 1140.  A small utility shed suffered some damage as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds of 80 mph impacted portions of central St. Louis County. The tornado developed around 5:06 p.m. 1.7 miles NW of the center of Creve Coeur, near the intersection of Kram Road and Olive Boulevard. The tornado ran nearly parallel to Timber Run Drive before turning northwest at Markaire Drive and impacted areas just southwest of North High School. The tornado crossed Fee Fee Road as it moved northwest and lifted over Seven Pines Drive at approximately 5:07 p.m. Several trees were damaged, power poles were snapped and a few homes were damaged due to falling limbs. Overall, the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 0.79 miles and a max path width of 50 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved eastward across the Mississippi River delta counties of extreme southeast Missouri. One of the storms produced a brief pulse of damaging wind. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central through southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a house, damaging the front porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms accompanied a cold front as it moved southeast across southeast Missouri. The lack of stronger vertical wind shear limited storm organization, favoring a predominantly multicellular storm mode. Even so, a few large hail events and a damaging downburst were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was snapped on County Road 731 just south of U.S. Highway 60. Other poles in the area were reported to be leaning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified on the southern periphery of a thunderstorm complex moving across southern Illinois. These storms were just to the cool side of an outflow boundary sagging south from the Illinois storms. Solar heating and destabilization resulted in enough of an increase in the storms for isolated strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 30 feet of tin roofing was blown off a barn.

Heat indices peaked around 105 degrees during the afternoon along and south of U.S. Highway 60. Some specific peak heat index values included 105 degrees at Poplar Bluff, 109 at Sikeston, and 103 at Cape Girardeau. The heat and humidity occurred just ahead of a weak cold front as it crossed the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri.

A storm system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. A band of snow began over portions of west central and central Missouri during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band then began moving southeast into the Interstate 44 corridor before diminishing during the mid morning hours on March 11th. An additional band of moderate to heavy snow then moved out of Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas into much of southern and south central Missouri during the early morning of March 11th and eventually came to an end from west to east from the late morning into mid afternoon hours. Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. Roads north of Interstate 44 and west of Highway 65 became snow covered during the heavier snowfall rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th. A couple slide-offs and accidents were reported. Numerous schools were also closed on March 11th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snow moved through during the late evening hours of March 10th into the mid morning hours of March 11th. Roads became snow covered. Generally 1 to 2.5 inches of snow accumulated across the county. The Iberia Cooperative Observer measured 1.5 inches of total snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bands of snow moved through the county. The first band of snow moved into the county during the overnight hours of March 10th before beginning to diminish over the county around 8 AM. The second snow band then moved into the county from the southwest and exited the county to the east during the late morning hours. Roads became partly to mostly snow covered during the second snow band, with snow on roadways quickly melting once snow began lightening up in the late morning hours. Between 0.5 to 1.5 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of locally heavy snow shifted southeast across parts of southeast Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours. Most places received one to two inches of snow, however an isolated 3-inch report was received. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less where the heaviest snow banding occurred. Since pavement temperatures were above freezing, most of the accumulation was on grassy areas. However, some slushy accumulations on roadways caused locally hazardous travel in the heaviest snow band. A series of accidents on Interstate 55 in Cape Girardeau County slowed or stopped traffic during the evening commute. Storm total snowfall reports included 3 inches near Oak Ridge and Millersville (Cape Girardeau County), 3 inches at Leeper (Wayne County), 2.5 inches near Greenville (Wayne County), 2 inches at Van Buren (Carter County) and Marble Hill (Bollinger County), and 1.5 inches at New Madrid. The snow was caused by a surface low pressure system which organized over the southern Plains and then tracked along a stationary front that was stretched along the Ohio River.

Minor river flooding began around midnight on the 1st of March on the Mississippi River near New Madrid. Low-lying fields and woodlands were inundated, primarily dormant cropland. The flooding was caused by several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms on the 17th of February, mainly in the Ohio River basin.

The heat and humidity occurred just ahead of a weak cold front as it crossed the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri.

A storm system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. A band of snow began over portions of west central and central Missouri during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band then began moving southeast into the Interstate 44 corridor before diminishing during the mid morning hours on March 11th. An additional band of moderate to heavy snow then moved out of Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas into much of southern and south central Missouri during the early morning of March 11th and eventually came to an end from west to east from the late morning into mid afternoon hours. Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. Roads north of Interstate 44 and west of Highway 65 became snow covered during the heavier snowfall rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th. A couple slide-offs and accidents were reported. Numerous schools were also closed on March 11th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bands of snow moved through the county. The first band of snow moved through during the early morning hours of March 11th before beginning to weaken over the county around 6 AM. The second snow band then immediately moved in from northeast Oklahoma and merged with the weakening initial snow band before exiting southeast of the county during the mid morning. Roads became snow covered. Between 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the county. Emergency management in Neosho reported total snow accumulations of 2.0 inches. The Shoal Creek Estates CoCoRaHs station MO-NW-4 reported a total snow accumulation of 1.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of locally heavy snow shifted southeast across parts of southeast Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours. Most places received one to two inches of snow, however an isolated 3-inch report was received. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less where the heaviest snow banding occurred. Since pavement temperatures were above freezing, most of the accumulation was on grassy areas. However, some slushy accumulations on roadways caused locally hazardous travel in the heaviest snow band. A series of accidents on Interstate 55 in Cape Girardeau County slowed or stopped traffic during the evening commute. Storm total snowfall reports included 3 inches near Oak Ridge and Millersville (Cape Girardeau County), 3 inches at Leeper (Wayne County), 2.5 inches near Greenville (Wayne County), 2 inches at Van Buren (Carter County) and Marble Hill (Bollinger County), and 1.5 inches at New Madrid. The snow was caused by a surface low pressure system which organized over the southern Plains and then tracked along a stationary front that was stretched along the Ohio River.

A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -31 degrees was reported at the Fort Leonard Wood AWOS station during the evening of December 22nd with a minimum temperature of -8 degrees. In addition, morning low temperatures on December 24th ranged from 3 degrees in Dixon to 5 degrees in St Robert, with minimum wind chills of -10 and -11 degrees, respectively.

A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys, producing a record-breaking early season heat wave. Numerous record highs and warm lows were established, including the first triple-digit temperatures experienced since 2016 in Cape Girardeau and since 2013 in Poplar Bluff. Cape Girardeau broke three daily high temperature records. Low temperatures in the lower 80's were observed in several locations, setting monthly and all-time records in some cases. Cape Girardeau tied its all-time warmest low of 81 degrees. Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees starting on June 12 and continued through June 16. The most oppressive day was June 13, when dewpoints in the mid 70's to around 80 produced heat index values of 110 to 115.

Small clusters of intense storms moved northeastward during the late afternoon and early evening. These pre-frontal storms were associated with a strengthening low-level jet and some cooling aloft. A surface warm front along the Arkansas border enhanced wind shear, contributing to an isolated very brief tornado despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy street flooding was reported in the city of Poplar Bluff.

Numerous reports of flooding were received to the east of Springfield, where some locations received over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flooding along Burnett Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. A band of snow began over portions of west central and central Missouri during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band then began moving southeast into the Interstate 44 corridor before diminishing during the mid morning hours on March 11th. An additional band of moderate to heavy snow then moved out of Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas into much of southern and south central Missouri during the early morning of March 11th and eventually came to an end from west to east from the late morning into mid afternoon hours. Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. Roads north of Interstate 44 and west of Highway 65 became snow covered during the heavier snowfall rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th. A couple slide-offs and accidents were reported. Numerous schools were also closed on March 11th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow band moved west to east across the county from the mid morning to early afternoon hours. Road impacts remained minimal due to snow falling on warm ground during the daylight hours. Widespread snow amounts ranged from 1 to 2.5 inches from south to north across the county, with the highest total snow accumulation of 2.5 inches reported by the West Plains Cooperative Observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -26 degrees was reported at the Versailles Mesonet station during the early morning of December 23rd with a minimum temperature of -9 degrees. In addition, the morning low temperature on December 24th was 2 degrees with a minimum wind chill of -10 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to Severe thunderstorms across Kansas and Missouri the evening of June 7th, 2022, continued to evolve into a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms as they moved into the Kansas City Metro area between 12:30 and 1 AM CDT. These storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the region as well as four confirmed tornadoes, one through the southern side of the Kansas City Metro, one in northeastern Jackson County Missouri and two near Louisburg, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were reported down in front of KWRT radio station in Boonville. There were also several trees down across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. A band of snow began over portions of west central and central Missouri during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band then began moving southeast into the Interstate 44 corridor before diminishing during the mid morning hours on March 11th. An additional band of moderate to heavy snow then moved out of Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas into much of southern and south central Missouri during the early morning of March 11th and eventually came to an end from west to east from the late morning into mid afternoon hours. Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. Roads north of Interstate 44 and west of Highway 65 became snow covered during the heavier snowfall rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th. A couple slide-offs and accidents were reported. Numerous schools were also closed on March 11th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snow moved through during the early to mid morning hours of March 11th. Roads became snow covered. Generally 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the county, with the highest amounts across the northern portion of the county. The Miller Cocorahs station MO-LW-4 reported a total snow accumulation of 2.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall across the Missouri Ozarks. A band of snow began over portions of west central and central Missouri during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band then began moving southeast into the Interstate 44 corridor before diminishing during the mid morning hours on March 11th. An additional band of moderate to heavy snow then moved out of Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas into much of southern and south central Missouri during the early morning of March 11th and eventually came to an end from west to east from the late morning into mid afternoon hours. Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bands of snow moved through the county. The first band of snow moved into the county during the early morning hours of March 11th before beginning to weaken over the county around 7 AM. The second snow band then immediately moved in from northeast Oklahoma and merged with the weakening initial snow band and then ended during the late morning. Roads became snow covered, particularly as the second snow band moved into the county. Generally between 0.5 to 2.5 inches of snow fell across the county, with the Cassville CoCoRaHs station MO-BY-25 measuring a snow total accumulation of 2.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of intense storms moved northeastward during the late afternoon and early evening. These pre-frontal storms were associated with a strengthening low-level jet and some cooling aloft. A surface warm front along the Arkansas border enhanced wind shear, contributing to an isolated very brief tornado despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video was relayed to the National Weather Service of an apparent tornado over an open field east of Neelyville. There was no known damage. The tornado was associated with a relatively weak shower or thunderstorm that did not exhibit severe characteristics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to Severe thunderstorms across Kansas and Missouri the evening of June 7th, 2022, continued to evolve into a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms as they moved into the Kansas City Metro area between 12:30 and 1 AM CDT. These storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the region as well as four confirmed tornadoes, one through the southern side of the Kansas City Metro, one in northeastern Jackson County Missouri and two near Louisburg, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was relayed via Sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated severe thunderstorm cells developed during the late afternoon hours in the warm sector of a surface low over east central Missouri. The storms developed in a marginally unstable but strongly sheared environment ahead of the trailing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shared on social media indicated one-inch diameter hail covered the ground near Poynor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple videos showed a narrow funnel cloud with some minor debris. The video was taken looking north from Portageville and west from Marston. There was damage to a carport and some power lines. The video and the damage report were relayed by local broadcast media. There was no apparent damage path on the ground. The short path track was based on videos combined with radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog along portions of Interstate 57 between the Mississippi River and Interstate 55 resulted in several fatalities (indirect) in a massive pile-up. The pile-up occurred on both sides of the interstate near Charleston, Missouri in Mississippi County. Approximately 50  vehicles were involved, including multiple tractor trailer rigs. Several vehicles erupted into flames. The visibility was below one-quarter mile at the closest automated airport observing site at Sikeston, Missouri. First responders at the scene reported visibility was around 50 feet. The first collision occurred when a semi tractor-trailer rear-ended another semi at the 13.4 mile marker southbound near Charleston. That accident caused a pile-up that included 15 vehicles. Two people were seriously injured, and two others received minor injuries. A second collision occurred almost simultaneously on the northbound side of the interstate, caused by the collision in the southbound lanes. A total of 14 people were injured, six seriously. The interstate was closed for nearly 24 hours. Dense fog blanketed extreme southeast Missouri from New Madrid to the south side of Cape Girardeau, extending west to Poplar Bluff. The dense fog occurred in a moist, light south wind flow on the back side of a high pressure ridge that extended from the Ohio Valley to the Gulf Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys, producing a record-breaking early season heat wave. Numerous record highs and warm lows were established, including the first triple-digit temperatures experienced since 2016 in Cape Girardeau and since 2013 in Poplar Bluff. Cape Girardeau broke three daily high temperature records. Low temperatures in the lower 80's were observed in several locations, setting monthly and all-time records in some cases. Cape Girardeau tied its all-time warmest low of 81 degrees. Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees starting on June 12 and continued through June 16. The most oppressive day was June 13, when dewpoints in the mid 70's to around 80 produced heat index values of 110 to 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms formed in eastern Kansas and pushed eastward into northwest Missouri. There were a couple mesovortex tornadoes within the line, one of which caused damage in St. Joseph. There was also some sporadic wind damage in the St Joseph area, but could not be confirmed as tornadic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage occurred at East Hills Mall, just upstream of a confirmed tornado. It is possible this damage was from the same circulation, but could not be confirmed as tornadic damage.

Snowfall generally ranged from around 0.5 to 3 inches across most of the area, though some portions of west central and central Missouri saw around 4-6 inches, as well as isolated 4 inch reports near Table Rock Lake. Roads north of Interstate 44 and west of Highway 65 became snow covered during the heavier snowfall rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th. A couple slide-offs and accidents were reported. Numerous schools were also closed on March 11th across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bands of snow moved through the county. The first band of snow moved into the county during the early morning hours of March 11th before beginning to diminish over the county around 7 AM. The second snow band then immediately moved in from northeast Oklahoma and merged with the weakening initial snow band before exiting southeast of the county during the mid morning. Roads became mostly snow covered during the second snow band, with snow on roadways quickly melting once snow began lightening up in the mid morning hours. Between 0.5 to 1.5 inches of snow fell across the county, with the highest snow totals over the north and western portion of the county. The Ash Grove Cooperative Observer reported a total snow accumulation of 1.2 inches. The CoCoRaHs station MO-GR-38, which is located 5.8 miles northeast of Willard, reported a total snow accumulation of 1.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog along portions of Interstate 57 between the Mississippi River and Interstate 55 resulted in several fatalities (indirect) in a massive pile-up. The pile-up occurred on both sides of the interstate near Charleston, Missouri in Mississippi County. Approximately 50  vehicles were involved, including multiple tractor trailer rigs. Several vehicles erupted into flames. The visibility was below one-quarter mile at the closest automated airport observing site at Sikeston, Missouri. First responders at the scene reported visibility was around 50 feet. The first collision occurred when a semi tractor-trailer rear-ended another semi at the 13.4 mile marker southbound near Charleston. That accident caused a pile-up that included 15 vehicles. Two people were seriously injured, and two others received minor injuries. A second collision occurred almost simultaneously on the northbound side of the interstate, caused by the collision in the southbound lanes. A total of 14 people were injured, six seriously. The interstate was closed for nearly 24 hours. Dense fog blanketed extreme southeast Missouri from New Madrid to the south side of Cape Girardeau, extending west to Poplar Bluff. The dense fog occurred in a moist, light south wind flow on the back side of a high pressure ridge that extended from the Ohio Valley to the Gulf Coast.
