A very strong low-level jet strengthened to around 80 knots, which set the stage for both strong gradient winds and thunderstorm winds. The highest measured peak gusts in non-thunderstorm winds were 49 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport and 47 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport. A few shingles were blown off a roof in Jackson.

Strong to Severe thunderstorms across Kansas and Missouri the evening of June 7th, 2022, continued to evolve into a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms as they moved into the Kansas City Metro area between 12:30 and 1 AM CDT. These storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the region as well as four confirmed tornadoes, one through the southern side of the Kansas City Metro, one in northeastern Jackson County Missouri and two near Louisburg, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were down just east of Whiteman AFB.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was some structural damage to a home and vehicle near Crain and Anderson Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys, producing a record-breaking early season heat wave. Numerous record highs and warm lows were established, including the first triple-digit temperatures experienced since 2016 in Cape Girardeau and since 2013 in Poplar Bluff. Cape Girardeau broke three daily high temperature records. Low temperatures in the lower 80's were observed in several locations, setting monthly and all-time records in some cases. Cape Girardeau tied its all-time warmest low of 81 degrees. Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees starting on June 12 and continued through June 16. The most oppressive day was June 13, when dewpoints in the mid 70's to around 80 produced heat index values of 110 to 115.

A line of thunderstorms formed in eastern Kansas and pushed eastward into northwest Missouri. There were a couple mesovortex tornadoes within the line, one of which caused damage in St. Joseph. There was also some sporadic wind damage in the St Joseph area, but could not be confirmed as tornadic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A QLCS mesovortex tornado formed along a progressive line of storms that moved through the St. Joseph area. EF-1 damage occurred at a residence on the northeast side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line of showers and thunderstorms produced a few damaging wind events. The line moved rapidly east across southeast Missouri. The strongly forced squall line was affected by very limited instability and moisture. Dew points were in the upper 50's ahead of the line. A very strong low level jet in excess of 80 knots resulted in a few damaging wind gusts. Heavy rain forced the St. Francis River to go above flood stage.  The river flooding consisted of minor flooding of low-lying fields. The line formed ahead of a strong cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure center passing north of the region. A very strong 500 mb shortwave pivoted east-northeast from the southern Plains, further enhancing the convection despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6-inch tree limb was down on a street.

Drought conditions improved throughout the month of August. The month began with extreme drought over the Ozark foothills, namely Carter and Ripley Counties, and severe drought as far east as the Poplar Bluff area and New Madrid County. By the end of the month, all areas were lifted out of drought into the abnormally dry category. Improvement in crop status and fire danger was rather temporary, however. A return to dry conditions would commence in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave moved east along a stationary front that was along the I-70 corridor. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread wind damage across Tipton. Numerous trees and tree limbs, as well as power lines were blown down. Some of the fallen trees and tree limbs caused damage to vehicles and houses. Also, four utility poles were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave moved east along a stationary front that was along the I-70 corridor. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large tree limbs in the Rainbow Hills and Hunters Run area of Apache Flats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread damage in and around French Village. Several garages were damaged and numerous trees and tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs and caused minor shingle damage to a couple of homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage from Bonne Terre to just east of Leadington. Numerous tree limbs were blown down. Also, several trees were blown down including a couple onto Highway D just east of Leadington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down onto an unoccupied police vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage was reported between Fredericktown and Marquand. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. MODOT crews were called to clear tree debris from Highways M, J, W and D.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree on the north side of town. The tree landed on a nearby garage causing moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -20 degrees was reported at the Lockwood Mesonet station during the late afternoon on December 22nd with a minimum temperature of -9 degrees measured during the late evening of December 22nd. In addition, the morning low temperature on December 24th measured 3 degrees at Lockwood, with a minimum wind chills of -2 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another month of below average moisture, especially in the drought stricken areas of western and central Missouri the severe (D2) drought continued. So far, there have been no reports of exceptional impacts, but as the drought continues impacts will increase. The drought continued into and through September 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: August saw little relief within a narrow strip of land just east of the Kansas City metro and just south of the Missouri River. Severe drought was declared in July by the UNL drought monitor, and given only a couple inches of rain for the month there was no improvement. The drought conditions continued into and through September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm in western Johnson County Missouri brought a hail report from a law enforcement officer near Pittsville, Missouri.
Overnight through the morning of November 15th some light snow fell, which accumulated on roadways causing a few automobile accidents. At least one of these accidents caused a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per MHP crash report, a eastbound pickup truck on Highway 150 just east of Highway 7 started sliding on the roadway, crossed the center, and collided head on with a small car traveling westbound. The driver of the of the car passed at the scene. ||MHP Crash Report: https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=220602365 .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight through the morning of November 15th some light snow fell, which accumulated on roadways causing a few automobile accidents. At least one of these accidents caused a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per MHP crash report, a vehicle was traveling northbound on Interstate 29 when it lost control on an icy road and crashed in traffic. A trailing large truck and trailer rig then hit the vehicle causing two occupants to be seriously injured. ||MHP crash report: https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=220602419 .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough deepened over the western US resulting in moist, southwest flow across the Mid-South. Diurnal heating, ample wind shear, and large amounts of instability sustained thunderstorms that formed over the Mid-South and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail in Wardell. Dime-sized hail just to the southwest in Bragg City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms across Arkansas during the morning hours of April 13, 2022. Storms eventually organized into a line that swept across the Mid-South. A strong low-level jet, large amounts of wind shear, and moderate amounts of instability supported severe thunderstorms across the Mid-South. Large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes were the result. Locally heavy rain also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles snapped on I-55 between Wardell and Hayward exits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed across parts of southeast Missouri during the overnight hours and burned off early in the morning. The dense fog was mainly along the Mississippi River counties from the Perryville area to Cape Girardeau, Sikeston, and Charleston. Visibility was at or below one-quarter mile. The fog formed under a light northeast wind flow from high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley.

The peak heat index ranged from 106 degrees at Doniphan in Ripley County to 113 degrees at the Perryville airport in Perry County. The heat occurred under a brisk southwest wind flow between high pressure over the central Gulf coast and low pressure over the upper Great Lakes.

Numerous slow-moving thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving cold front. The front extended from Lake Michigan southwest to the Ozark Plateau of southwest Missouri. Locally heavy rain under these storms caused flash flooding of roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some streets in and near Van Buren were flooded.

Late in the evening of February 1 light to moderate snow formed across western and central Missouri. The heavier band of snow set up more in central Missouri, where roughly 6 to 12 inches of snow fell. Within that 6-12 inch band the area around Marshall and Moberly, Missouri and areas to the east received the higher amounts within that range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 6 to 8 inches of snow fell predominantly in the southern portion of Macon County overnight on the 1st, going into the early morning hours on the 2nd of February. More snow moved in a few hours later, but did not appear to contribute significantly to the totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low and its attendant cold front moved across the area on the morning of August 3rd. A line of storms developed ahead of the front and one of these storms produced damaging winds as it moved into Scotland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A law enforcement officer reports that thunderstorm wind gusts damaged a car port. They also reported that very little to no rain occurred at the time of the wind gust which may have been a microburst. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 8 to 12 inches of snow fell overnight on the 1st, going into the early morning hours on the 2nd of February. More snow moved in a few hours later, but did not appear to contribute significantly to the totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic front swept through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening on December 22nd. Temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in the first hour after the frontal passage. As temperatures plummeted rain changed to sleet and snow and quickly accumulated on area roads. A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across the Missouri Bootheel with numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month of June continued into July allowing for the development of Extreme Drought (D3) across portions of southern Missouri. Drought conditions deepened first across portions of south central Missouri to Severe Drought (D2) by the 5th of the month and expanded across the Ozarks. By the 19th, Severe Drought expanded across most of the Ozarks and into portions of central Missouri with Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Oregon and Howell Counties. With little to no rainfall occurring through the 26th of the month, Extreme Drought (D3) expanded significantly across most of southwestern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated over half of the Ozarks area as being in extreme (D3) drought, including Cedar County. The rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over northern and eastern portions of the Ozarks with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms organized into a line of thunderstorms that produced scattered pockets of wind damage across southeast Missouri, mainly along and north of U.S. Highway 60. The storms occurred along a cold front that extended from central Indiana southwest to the Ozark Mountains of Arkansas. A very moist and unstable air mass supported the storms over Missouri, where mixed-layer cape generally exceeded 2000 j/kg. Southwest 850 mb winds increased to over 30 knots, further aiding storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medium to large-size tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated minor wind damage accompanied several severe storms. A weak frontal boundary was draped from the Great Lakes region to the Ozarks of Missouri. A small complex of thunderstorms formed along this boundary during the heat of the late afternoon and early evening hours. Surface dew points in the mid to upper 70's contributed to a very unstable atmosphere. Mixed layer capes were over 3000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped 110 degrees across southeast Missouri in the afternoon. Peak heat indices ranged from 110 at Van Buren in Carter County to 114 at Poplar Bluff and 117 at Perryville. A hot and humid southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front brought temperatures up to the mid to upper 90's. Dew points were exceptionally high, with readings in the lower 80's at most stations. Early morning low temperatures were quite warm. Both Poplar Bluff and Cape Girardeau broke daily warm low temperature records. The low was 78 at Poplar Bluff and 79 at Cape Girardeau.

By the 19th, Severe Drought expanded across most of the Ozarks and into portions of central Missouri with Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Oregon and Howell Counties. With little to no rainfall occurring through the 26th of the month, Extreme Drought (D3) expanded significantly across most of southwestern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated over half of the Ozarks area as being in extreme (D3) drought, including Lawrence County. The rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over northern and eastern portions of the Ozarks with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated severe thunderstorms occurred during the evening hours. The storms occurred on the warm side of a weak frontal boundary that extended from central Illinois to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri. Wind shear was rather weak, but strong instability lingered through the evening hours. Mixed-layer capes were still around 2000 j/kg late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down on County Road 125 south of U.S. Highway 60. A large tree was blocking part of State Route C. Other tree damage occurred near the intersection of State Route C and County Road 3280.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms organized into a line of thunderstorms that produced scattered pockets of wind damage across southeast Missouri, mainly along and north of U.S. Highway 60. The storms occurred along a cold front that extended from central Indiana southwest to the Ozark Mountains of Arkansas. A very moist and unstable air mass supported the storms over Missouri, where mixed-layer cape generally exceeded 2000 j/kg. Southwest 850 mb winds increased to over 30 knots, further aiding storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were down along Route M to the northwest of Van Buren. Roof damage occurred to a business in Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another episode of hazardous heat impacted most of southeast Missouri. Peak heat indices on the afternoons of the 26th and 27th were around 105 degrees. Hot and humid southwest winds persisted ahead of a cold front as it moved east from the Plains toward the Mississippi Valley. Actual high temperatures were in the lower 90's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred over the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. The storm formed during the heat of the day along a well-defined outflow boundary from a thunderstorm complex over Illinois earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ripley County Sheriff Department reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions developed over parts of the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri, namely Carter and Ripley Counties. Severe drought crept east into the Poplar Bluff area, as well as extreme southeast Missouri around New Madrid County. Crops showed signs of stress. Some farmers were feeding hay to supplement where pastures were struggling. Fire danger increased, and a burn ban was imposed in Ripley County. Between 40 and 60 percent of topsoil moisture was considered short or very short. July monthly rainfall was well below normal. It was the 9th driest July on record at Cape Girardeau. Monthly rainfall was one to 2.5 inches below normal across much of the drought area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas south of a stationary front over the Missouri Valley warmed into the mid 80's to the mid 90's. The air mass was very moist, with dew points in the lower to mid 70's. Instability was quite strong, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Minimal wind shear inhibited thunderstorm organization. A storm pulsed up to severe levels, producing isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped four feet off the ground at the intersection of Highway 114 and Highway KK just south of Essex. There was also a lot of small and minor tree damage in Essex, with smaller branches broken off. Four large branches were broken in Penermon and the area immediately southwest of town.

A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-FSA-065 1.8 miles northwest of Salem reported 1.0 inch of snowfall. Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and dangerous, with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds.   Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season.

Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -22 degrees was reported at the Nevada Mesonet station during the evening of December 22nd with a minimum temperature of -4 degrees. In addition, the morning low temperature on December 24th was 5 degrees with a minimum wind chill of -5 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month of June continued into July allowing for the development of Extreme Drought (D3) across portions of southern Missouri. Drought conditions deepened first across portions of south central Missouri to Severe Drought (D2) by the 5th of the month and expanded across the Ozarks. By the 19th, Severe Drought expanded across most of the Ozarks and into portions of central Missouri with Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Oregon and Howell Counties. With little to no rainfall occurring through the 26th of the month, Extreme Drought (D3) expanded significantly across most of southwestern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated over half of the Ozarks area as being in extreme (D3) drought, including Webster County. The rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over northern and eastern portions of the Ozarks with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave gripped southeast Missouri for several days. From the 4th to the 8th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 103 to 119 degrees. The hottest day in southeast Missouri was the 5th, when the heat index peaked between 111 and 119 at all mesonet and airport sites. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Both Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff  set a daily record warm low temperature of 78 degrees on the 6th. Both Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff reached an actual high temperature of 100 degrees on the 5th. The heat resulted in two fatalities at a home in Gideon in New Madrid County. A couple in their 50's both passed away in their home, primarily due to heat exposure. Three dogs were also found deceased. The house had no air conditioning and was not well-ventilated.

An estimated 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the county.  Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and dangerous, with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds.   Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms fired up during the heat of the day across southeast Missouri. This activity developed along a weak cold front and in the vicinity of a weak surface low centered over the mid Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with mixed-layer capes from 1500 to 2500 J/kg. Wind shear was sufficiently strong for an isolated storm to become severe, with damaging wind gusts. Effective bulk shear was around 30 to 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered four-inch tree limbs were down around town, along with some power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated severe thunderstorms occurred during the evening hours. The storms occurred on the warm side of a weak frontal boundary that extended from central Illinois to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri. Wind shear was rather weak, but strong instability lingered through the evening hours. Mixed-layer capes were still around 2000 j/kg late in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month of June continued into July allowing for the development of Extreme Drought (D3) across portions of southern Missouri. Drought conditions deepened first across portions of south central Missouri to Severe Drought (D2) by the 5th of the month and expanded across the Ozarks. By the 19th, Severe Drought expanded across most of the Ozarks and into portions of central Missouri with Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Oregon and Howell Counties. With little to no rainfall occurring through the 26th of the month, Extreme Drought (D3) expanded significantly across most of southwestern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated over half of the Ozarks area as being in Severe (D2) drought, including St. Clair County. The rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over northern and eastern portions of the Ozarks with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -31 degrees was reported at the Pleasant Hope mesonet station during the evening of December 22nd with a minimum temperature of -7 degrees. In addition, morning low temperatures on December 24th ranged from 3 degrees at the Bolivar Elementary School to 5 degrees in Pleasant Hope, with minimum wind chills ranging from -14 degrees at the Bolivar AWOS to -10 degrees in Pleasant Hope.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-TX-44 approximately 3.6 miles south of Summersville reported 1.5 inches of snowfall. Another snow report from CoCoRaHS observer MO-TX-41 approximately 1 mile northeast of Houston reported 1.5 inches of snow.  Snowfall amounts may have been underestimated as nearby observations at Fort Leonard Wood contained blowing snow for 15 hours.  Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and dangerous, with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds.   Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms organized into a line of thunderstorms that produced scattered pockets of wind damage across southeast Missouri, mainly along and north of U.S. Highway 60. The storms occurred along a cold front that extended from central Indiana southwest to the Ozark Mountains of Arkansas. A very moist and unstable air mass supported the storms over Missouri, where mixed-layer cape generally exceeded 2000 j/kg. Southwest 850 mb winds increased to over 30 knots, further aiding storm development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions continued across portions of southwestern Missouri. Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of November. By the last week of November, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 34 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 7 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 3 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Morgan County by the end of November. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally Dry (D0) drought conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 3 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event. Several accidents occurred across the county due to slick snow packed roads. One accident with injuries occurred on I-55 north bound near mile marker 157. The occupant sustained moderate injuries and was transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward shift in the Westerlies forced a slow moving front to move southeastward into the Ozarks Region on the 8th. The front then stalled and interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce heavy rainfall and severe storms over the area through the early afternoon of the 9th. The strongest storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 9th, producing widespread damaging wind gusts which blew down trees and powerlines, and hail to the size of ping pong balls. The hardest hit areas were along and south of Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield to south of Lebanon. Rainfall accumulations of two to over four inches occurred from the Lake of the Ozarks to Stockton Lake and then southward toward Springfield, Mount Vernon and Neosho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down at the intersection of Farm Roads 97 and 182.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon and early evening as a very moist and unstable air mass interacted with upper level energy and an approaching cold front. The strongest storms developed during the early and mid-afternoon of the 29th, producing sporadic wind damage and torrential rainfall across the southern half of the area. Parts of Springfield received over three inches of rain within a period of around one to two hours. This produced flash flooding in Springfield, Nixa and Marshfield. Wind damage was also|reported with storms over south central and southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down across Sunshine and Virginia Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 10 inches of rain fell in a 6 to 10 hour period causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded including Highway A at the intersection with Highway W, Morsey Center Road about half a mile east of Highway A, and State Highway 47 just south of Powerline Road. Culvert was washed away on Pin Oak Church Road just east of y intersection near New Truxton Road, making the road impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward shift in the Westerlies forced a slow moving front to move southeastward into the Ozarks Region on the 8th. The front then stalled and interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce heavy rainfall and severe storms over the area through the early afternoon of the 9th. The strongest storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 9th, producing widespread damaging wind gusts which blew down trees and powerlines, and hail to the size of ping pong balls. The hardest hit areas were along and south of Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield to south of Lebanon. Rainfall accumulations of two to over four inches occurred from the Lake of the Ozarks to Stockton Lake and then southward toward Springfield, Mount Vernon and Neosho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wilsons Creek at Old Limey Road was flooded and impassable due to heavy rain and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated the extreme southern portion of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder in Moderate (D1) drought or Abnormally dry (D0). The rainfall received during the month provided only isolated relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions continued across portions of southwestern Missouri. Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of November. By the last week of November, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 34 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 7 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 3 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Morgan County by the end of November. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Moderate (D1) drought conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 2 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon and early evening as a very moist and unstable air mass interacted with upper level energy and an approaching cold front. Parts of Springfield received over three inches of rain within a period of around one to two hours. This produced flash flooding in Springfield, Nixa and Marshfield. Wind damage was also|reported with storms over south central and southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down along or on Highways 76 and Y in the Railey Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of October. By the last week of October, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 84 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 23 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 6 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across the much of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of October. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northern and western portions of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder of the county in Severe (D2) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions continued across portions of southwestern Missouri. Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of November. By the last week of November, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 34 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 7 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 3 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Morgan County by the end of November. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally Dry (D0) drought conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops.

Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms, extending from St. Louis metro area to southeastern Missouri, moved eastward through the afternoon and early evening hours. With precipitable water values over 2 inches and warm cloud depths up to 14,000 feet thick, these storms produced very heavy rainfall, up to five inches, in a short period of time, between 2 pm and 7 pm on July 28th. Also, the soil in the area was quite saturated due to the catastrophic flash flooding two days prior. This caused another round of flash flooding in portions of St. Louis County, St. Louis city, Jefferson County in Missouri and St. Clair County in Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches fell in a three hour period causing flash flooding across the northern half of the city of St. Louis. Numerous roads were flooded including Washington Avenue near the intersection with Walton Avenue, south bound Skinker near Forest Park, Lindell Boulevard near Vandeventer Avenue, South Vandeventer Avenue just south of I-64 and Kingshighway Boulevard between Natural Bridge Avenue and St. Louis Avenue. Flash flooding in the 4700 block of Washington Avenue flooded a local daycare. The fire department had to rescue 15 children and 3 adults from the facility. No injuries were reported. Also, several dozen motorists had to be rescued from their flooded vehicles. Five city schools were damaged due to flooding. The flooding affected schools from north St. Louis to south. Soldan High School had at least 2 feet of water in the cafeteria on the basement floor. At least 4 businesses sustained water damage from the flooding in the Central West End. Both directions of Interstate 64 at Hampton Avenue and eastbound Interstate 70 at Union Boulevard were closed for part of the afternoon due to flooding.

Rainfall accumulations for the 48-hour period ending at 7 AM on the 17th exceeded two inches. Some minor flooding was reported near the Lake of the Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred along Spring Cove Road. One vehicle had water inside as it was stuck in four feet of water. Several other roads were impacted by flood waters.

Vertical wind shear was sufficient enough for several strong to severe storms to develop from Springfield eastward toward Rolla and Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large, up to eight inch diameter, limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon and early evening as a very moist and unstable air mass interacted with upper level energy and an approaching cold front. The strongest storms developed during the early and mid-afternoon of the 29th, producing sporadic wind damage and torrential rainfall across the southern half of the area. Parts of Springfield received over three inches of rain within a period of around one to two hours. This produced flash flooding in Springfield, Nixa and Marshfield. Wind damage was also|reported with storms over south central and southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Nixa via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southward from the upper Midwest into northern and central Missouri on the morning of the 3rd and sparked showers and thunderstorms over central Missouri  during the afternoon and evening. The stronger storms produced up to quarter sized hail and damaging wind gusts in Laclede, Phelps and Webster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported to fall for over a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved northeast across the Missouri Ozarks during the evening hours of November 14, 2022 into the early morning hours of November 15, 2022. Precipitation began as a brief rain and snow mix during the first half of the evening before changing over to all snow. Some drizzle occurred after the snow ended during the early morning of November 15, 2022, but air and road temperatures at or above freezing at this time prevented any freezing drizzle from occurring. Snow was wetter with around an 8 to 1 snow ratio. Most widespread accumulations were less than two inches across the area, except for some locations across central Missouri and the Lake of the Ozarks region that saw up to two to four inches in a heavier snow band. This early season snowfall was the second snowfall of the season, but the first impactful one. Roads across central Missouri and the Lake of the Ozarks region became snow covered and slippery during the mid to late evening due to the higher snow rates, with several slide offs occurring. Road conditions improved overnight after the heavier snow bands exited the area due to near or slightly above freezing road and air temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain and snow mix quickly changed over to snow during the evening hours of November 14th. Widespread snowfall ranging from 1 to 1.5 inches fell across the county. The highest amount of 1.5 inches was reported 1 mile southeast of Old Linn Creek by amateur radio. Roads became snow covered and slippery in the county during the bouts of higher snowfall rates, with several slide offs reported on Highway 42 entering Kaiser and Osage Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward shift in the Westerlies forced a slow moving front to move southeastward into the Ozarks Region on the 8th. The front then stalled and interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce heavy rainfall and severe storms over the area through the early afternoon of the 9th. The strongest storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 9th, producing widespread damaging wind gusts which blew down trees and powerlines, and hail to the size of ping pong balls. The hardest hit areas were along and south of Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield to south of Lebanon. Rainfall accumulations of two to over four inches occurred from the Lake of the Ozarks to Stockton Lake and then southward toward Springfield, Mount Vernon and Neosho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions continued across portions of southwestern Missouri. Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of November. By the last week of November, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 34 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 7 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 3 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of November though rainfall through the month allowed for some improvement in drought conditions. The US Drought Monitor indicated the far southwestern corner of the county as remaining in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder of the county in Severe (D2) Drought to Abnormally Dry (D0) in the far northern portion of the county. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture including row crops and hay. Farm and stock ponds saw some improvements ground water levels and reservoirs. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 11 inches of rain fell between a 6 to 10 hour period causing flash flooding. The heaviest rain was across the far southern portions of Lincoln County. Numerous roads were flooded including Route J south of Troy, Route C west of Old Monroe, and Route MM. Also, a culvert was washed out on Route U between Fred Norton Road and Giles road. Several water rescues were performed. One driver was northbound on Highway J just south of Big Creek in the early morning hours and could not see the flooded roadway in front of her. Before she knew it she was in the water and could not back out of it. The vehicle began to float and fill with water. She called her husband, told him where she was at and asked for him to call 911. She managed to get out of one of her back doors and slip out, grabbing a branch just before her vehicle was completely submerged. It took two and a half hours for the fire rescue to get a boat to the location and rescue her from the tree she was clinging to. She was uninjured. With the other rescues, no one was injured as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary developed and moved southward into southern Missouri before interacting with a low level jet to produce additional heavy rainfall and isolated wind damage during the overnight hours of the 3rd into the morning of the 4th. Rainfall accumulations were highest over central and south central Missouri, ranging from two to four inches along and east of a line from Warsaw to West Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive wind damage occurred to five homes, multiple power lines and poles, and trees.     | A barn was also destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daily and monthly rainfall records were set at the region's airports. Rain associated with cold fronts and thunderstorms produced significant rainfall across the region on the 4th, 16th, 28th and 29th. The highest monthly precipitation totals for August 2022 observed from rain gauges within the NWS Springfield Cooperative Observation Network (COOP), and Community Collaborative Rain, Hail and Snow Network (CoCoRaHS) stations and ranged from 6.01 to as much as 8.31 inches for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall recorded over 24 hours occurred across the Ozarks on the 4th, 9th, 26th and 29th. Locations recorded rainfall via Mesonet, manual and automated gauges. Rainfall reading were as little as 1 inch to as much as 5.23 inches in a 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Professional Building at Cherry Street and Kimbrough had water in the first floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated much of the southern parts of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder in Severe (D2) drought. The rainfall received during the month provided little relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Douglas County by the end of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated that Abnormally dry (D0) conditions remained after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Much of the losses from row crops will not be felt until the fall when harvest occurs.

The front then stalled and interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce heavy rainfall and severe storms over the area through the early afternoon of the 9th. The strongest storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 9th, producing widespread damaging wind gusts which blew down trees and powerlines, and hail to the size of ping pong balls. The hardest hit areas were along and south of Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield to south of Lebanon. Rainfall accumulations of two to over four inches occurred from the Lake of the Ozarks to Stockton Lake and then southward toward Springfield, Mount Vernon and Neosho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rain caused flash flooding across Hines Street near Brookline Fire Station 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms, extending from St. Louis metro area to southeastern Missouri, moved eastward through the afternoon and early evening hours. With precipitable water values over 2 inches and warm cloud depths up to 14,000 feet thick, these storms produced very heavy rainfall, up to five inches, in a short period of time, between 2 pm and 7 pm on July 28th. Also, the soil in the area was quite saturated due to the catastrophic flash flooding two days prior. This caused another round of flash flooding in portions of St. Louis County, St. Louis city, Jefferson County in Missouri and St. Clair County in Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree branch onto the roof of a house. The roof sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw some improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of October. By the last week of October, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 84 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 23 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Significant Drought (D4), by the end of the month 23 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Extreme Drought (D3) in 6 counties. The Significant Drought (D4) had improved falling to Extreme Drought (D3) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across the much of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of October. The US Drought Monitor indicated the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of 2022 started off a bit dry, but by mid-July D2 drought was declared for Jackson, Lafayette, Johnson, Saline, Pettis, Cooper and Howard Counties. The drought for these counties continued into and through August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of Tuesday, July 26th, 2022, a complex of training thunderstorms set up roughly along the I-70 corridor in Missouri and I-64 corridor in Illinois. Several rounds of thunderstorms with rainfall rates exceeding 2in/hr affected this area, including the St. Louis metropolitan area, through the early hours of July 26th and into the morning commute. Upwards of 11 of rain fell over the course of roughly 8 hours in an axis from Hawk Point, MO to St. Peters, MO according to radar-estimated rainfall products and several CoCoRaHS/spotter reports. A longer axis of over 8 of rain fell from northern Montgomery County in Missouri to St. Clair County in Illinois, causing multiple swift water rescues and scores of flooded interstates and homes across the St. Louis metropolitan area. St. Louis-Lambert International Airport also observed a new all-time record for daily precipitation: 8.64 of rain fell since midnight Central Standard Time (Daylight Savings Time is not used for climatological record-keeping). This value broke the previous record of 6.85, which occurred on August 20th, 1915 due to the remnants of the Galveston 1915 Hurricane. The storm-total rainfall was 9.07, but spans two days. Two fatalities were reported: one in St. Louis City, where a man drowned in his car near Skinker Blvd, and another in Hazelwood where a man drowned attempting to flee his flooded truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 13 inches of rain fell in a 6 to 10 hour period causing widespread flash flooding. The axis of heaviest rain extended from just north of Wentzville east southeastward through northern portions of O'Fallon through St. Peters and into St. Charles. Numerous roads were flooded throughout St. Charles County. Interstate 70 was flooded with up to five feet of water around the intersection with Mid Rivers Mall Drive. Fire and rescue searched and cleared several dozen vehicles in this area and rescued 8 people from the rising waters. Also, in the area of Harry S. Truman Blvd and Premier Parkway, several drivers were rescued from their flooded vehicles. In the area of Mexico Road and Spencer Road, multiple vehicles were trapped by rapidly rising waters of Spencer Creek. One vehicle in this area was swept off the roadway into Spencer Creek. The driver escaped but was clinging to a tree in the fast moving water that was over 8 feet deep. He was rescued and was not injured. Between 1 am and 11 am, 87 people were evacuated from homes or businesses in St. Charles County. One hundred seventeen buildings were damaged from the flooding. Forty Nine of them sustained major damage, 46 sustained moderate damage and 22 buildings sustained minor damage. So far damage is estimated to be around $10 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary developed and moved southward into southern Missouri before interacting with a low level jet to produce additional heavy rainfall and isolated wind damage during the overnight hours of the 3rd into the morning of the 4th. Rainfall accumulations were highest over central and south central Missouri, ranging from two to four inches along and east of a line from Warsaw to West Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route WW at Archer Creek was closed and impassable.

Parts of Springfield received over three inches of rain within a period of around one to two hours. This produced flash flooding in Springfield, Nixa and Marshfield. Wind damage was also|reported with storms over south central and southwest Missouri.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Cedar County by the end of November. The US Drought Monitor indicated that moderate (D1) drought conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized downburst downed multiple trees at Highway 42 and Bear Creek Road, as well as at Highways 42 and 134 blocking the road. Trees were also downed at the Lee C Fine Airport. Damage occurred to two planes at the airport and the AWOS went offline due to loss of power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward shift in the Westerlies forced a slow moving front to move southeastward into the Ozarks Region on the 8th. The front then stalled and interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce heavy rainfall and severe storms over the area through the early afternoon of the 9th. The strongest storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 9th, producing widespread damaging wind gusts which blew down trees and powerlines, and hail to the size of ping pong balls. The hardest hit areas were along and south of Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield to south of Lebanon. Rainfall accumulations of two to over four inches occurred from the Lake of the Ozarks to Stockton Lake and then southward toward Springfield, Mount Vernon and Neosho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light damage occurred to a home with some shingles removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over most of the Missouri Ozarks during the month, thanks to periods of heavy rainfall from early August to late August. By the last week of August, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 35 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to extreme drought (D3). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 24 counties in D2 (Severe Drought) to (D3 extreme Drought), by the end of the month only 8 counties remained in (D2) Severe drought or greater.  The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of August. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northern half of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder in Severe (D2) Drought. The rainfall received during the month provided little relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon and early evening as a very moist and unstable air mass interacted with upper level energy and an approaching cold front. The strongest storms developed during the early and mid-afternoon of the 29th, producing sporadic wind damage and torrential rainfall across the southern half of the area. Parts of Springfield received over three inches of rain within a period of around one to two hours. This produced flash flooding in Springfield, Nixa and Marshfield. Wind damage was also|reported with storms over south central and southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were reported down or split with numerous large limbs down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Mesonet station located approximately six miles west of Steele measured a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very early season snowfall blanketed southeast Missouri during the early morning hours. The highest accumulations ranged from three to four inches in the hilly terrain from around Van Buren to Greenville to western Bollinger and Perry Counties. Less than an inch fell in the Cape Girardeau area, as well as the southern border counties from Doniphan east to New Madrid. Most of the snow fell in just 1 to 3 hours. Visibility was below one mile in the heaviest snow bands. Because temperatures had been in the 60's, 70's, and even lower 80's in the days preceding the snow, pavement temperatures were such that the snow melted on roads. However, snowfall rates were high enough to briefly cover bridges and overpasses, resulting in a few accidents. This unseasonably early snowfall was the third earliest snow event of an inch or more at Paducah, Kentucky. The earliest was on the day before Halloween of 1993 (2 inches), and the second earliest was on November 11, 2019 (1.2 inches). The snow occurred in cold northerly flow behind a strong cold front and ahead of a deep 500 mb trough from the upper Missississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau.

Drought conditions worsened again in September after a period of wet weather in August. As the month progressed, the area of moderate drought expanded to include areas along and south of a Cape Girardeau to Marble Hill line. These parts of southeast Missouri experienced an abnormally dry month, ranking in the top 10 driest Septembers on record. Less than an inch fell in much of this region. At Cape Girardeau, September was the 4th driest September on record, with only 0.63 inch. Poplar Bluff recorded its 6th driest September, with only 0.41 inch. Monthly departures from normal of 2 to 4 inches were commonplace. Field and brush fire danger increased as grass turned brown. Bans on outdoor burning were initiated in Cape Girardeau County by the end of the month. The dryness of the summer as a whole, combined with periods of excessive heat, took a toll on crop yields. The corn crop was more adversely affected than the soybean crop because of the timing of the drought earlier in the summer. Soybean yields were not affected, but corn yields were forecast to be up to 25 percent lower than 2021.

Widespread dense fog blanketed areas north of a line from Doniphan to Poplar Bluff to Dexter and New Madrid during the night-time and early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less at times. High pressure centered over southern Illinois produced conditions favorable for dense fog.

Over most of southeast Missouri except the Ozark foothills (north and west of Poplar Bluff), strong south winds gusted from 40 to around 50 mph. The highest measured wind gust was at the Cape Girardeau airport, where winds peaked at 52 mph. The highest gust at the Poplar Bluff airport was 45 mph. The strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed south from a low pressure center over the upper Mississippi Valley.

A cold front moved southeast through the region with storms developing along and ahead of the boundary. Some of the storms became severe with isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds over portions of northeast Missouri as well as west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused moderate damage to a farm outbuilding.

Drought conditions improved slightly across southeast Missouri during the month. The drought severity improved from extreme to severe across the southeast corner of the state, from Cape Girardeau and Dexter east to the Mississippi River, including New Madrid. The Ozark foothills and Perryville area improved from severe to moderate drought. The remainder of southeast Missouri remained in severe drought. Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which is normally reserved for the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. Wildfire danger remained high at times during the month. However, periodic light rainfalls allowed all counties to lift bans on outdoor burning. Even so, rainfall deficits were still 5 to 7 inches below normal for the fall (September 1 to November 30). One of the most significant impacts was very low water levels on the Mississippi River. Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, however rain improved river levels sufficiently for navigation to fully resume by the end of the month.

Minimal rain occurred in the drought area through the month of September. So, expanding and worsening drought was indicated by the UNL US drought monitor. The general area of the drought from July to September persisted in drought, but with some expansion into neighboring counties. A couple counties saw conditions worsen to extreme D3, which also carried into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September saw little relief within a narrow strip of land just east of the Kansas City metro and just south of the Missouri River. Severe drought was declared in July by the UNL drought monitor, and given minimal rain through September there was no improvement. The drought conditions continued into and through October.

Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which is normally reserved for the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. Wildfire danger remained high at times during the month. However, periodic light rainfalls allowed all counties to lift bans on outdoor burning. Even so, rainfall deficits were still 5 to 7 inches below normal for the fall (September 1 to November 30). One of the most significant impacts was very low water levels on the Mississippi River. Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, however rain improved river levels sufficiently for navigation to fully resume by the end of the month.

Minimal rain occurred in the drought area through the month of September. So, expanding and worsening drought was indicated by the UNL US drought monitor. The general area of the drought from July to September persisted in drought, but with some expansion into neighboring counties. A couple counties saw conditions worsen to extreme D3, which also carried into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September saw little relief within a narrow strip of land just east of the Kansas City metro and just south of the Missouri River. Severe drought was declared in July by the UNL drought monitor, and given minimal rain through September there was no improvement. The drought conditions continued into and through October.

Scattered thunderstorms formed during the heat of the day along a cold front as it sagged into southeast Missouri. The storms formed within a corridor of 1000 to 1500 J/kg mixed-layer capes across the Ozarks into extreme western Tennessee. As thunderstorms spread southeast within this moderately unstable air mass, there was sufficient wind shear to support a few organized storm cells that produced large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported just west of Ellsinore. Half-dollar size hail broke a house window in Ellsinore.
