Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on the evening of September 18, 2022. Storms initially developed near the Iowa/Missouri border, with one storm near Bloomfield IA producing a well defined wall cloud before weakening. Storms developed and increased in strength just east of the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros as they slowly tracked east around the Interstate 80 corridor. Several large hail reports were received around Durant to south of Tipton, with the largest report being 2.5 inches. Storms also produced small hail and wind damage in the Quad Cities before spreading southeast into Henry County IL. Additional storms developed along the Iowa/Missouri/Illinois tri-state area, with several tornado warnings issued. Damage was reported in southern Hancock County near Tioga, and continued south into Adams County, although no tornadoes were confirmed.

Due to the lack of widespread beneficial rain the ongoing severe drought has expanded into a few counties. Thus far the impacts have been minimal, but the drought conditions expanded and continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to ongoing lack of rain across the area the severe (D2) drought has expanded into Cass County. So far there have been little to no reports of impacts, but the drought continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog blanketed a few counties along the Mississippi River, from Perryville south to Sikeston, Charleston, and East Prairie. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less during the early morning hours. High pressure extending from the central Gulf coast through the Tennessee and lower Ohio Valleys resulted in conditions favorable for the fog.

Over most of southeast Missouri except the Ozark foothills (north and west of Poplar Bluff), strong south winds gusted from 40 to around 50 mph. The highest measured wind gust was at the Cape Girardeau airport, where winds peaked at 52 mph. The highest gust at the Poplar Bluff airport was 45 mph. The strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed south from a low pressure center over the upper Mississippi Valley.

A powerful cold front swept through the region, with wind chills dropping to 20 to 30 below zero. These were the coldest wind chills observed in over 25 years across the region. Paducah, KY dropped down to a -21 wind chill, which was the coldest since February 3, 1996. The most extreme wind chills were from the evening of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd, but wind chills as late as the morning of December 24th were often below minus 10. Actual air temperatures only reached the single digits above zero on the 23rd, which followed morning lows around 5 below. Peak wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were observed during this event as well. The peak gust at Cape Girardeau was 44 mph on the evening of the 22nd. Some power outages were reported in Cape Girardeau, where a power line was down and a substation transformer caught fire. The extreme cold was indirectly responsible for a number of structure fires caused by supplemental heating sources, such as space heaters. There were numerous reports of frozen water pipes that burst and flooded homes and other buildings. In Cape Girardeau, an underground 6-inch water main burst, causing a loss of water pressure to a neighborhood. Another water main break occurred in Jackson. There were reports of frozen water meters in Cape Girardeau. On top of the extreme cold, one to three inches of snow fell. The highest amounts were generally along and south of a line from Van Buren in Carter County through Greenville in Wayne County to Benton in Scott County, where around 3 inches fell. Snowfall amounts elsewhere were from 1 to 2 inches. This snow and the accompanying flash freeze heavily impacted travel across the region. Despite relatively light traffic, there were numerous traffic mishaps. The Missouri Highway Patrol in southeast Missouri responded to 80 stranded motorists, 33 non-injury crashes, and 4 injury crashes during the winter storm.

A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 2 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event. Several accidents occurred across the county due to slick snow packed roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region. A few strong to severe storms developed with reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs in St. Charles.

The most extreme wind chills were from the evening of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd, but wind chills as late as the morning of December 24th were often below minus 10. Actual air temperatures only reached the single digits above zero on the 23rd, which followed morning lows around 5 below. Peak wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were observed during this event as well. The peak gust at Cape Girardeau was 44 mph on the evening of the 22nd. Some power outages were reported in Cape Girardeau, where a power line was down and a substation transformer caught fire. The extreme cold was indirectly responsible for a number of structure fires caused by supplemental heating sources, such as space heaters. There were numerous reports of frozen water pipes that burst and flooded homes and other buildings. In Cape Girardeau, an underground 6-inch water main burst, causing a loss of water pressure to a neighborhood. Another water main break occurred in Jackson. There were reports of frozen water meters in Cape Girardeau. On top of the extreme cold, one to three inches of snow fell. The highest amounts were generally along and south of a line from Van Buren in Carter County through Greenville in Wayne County to Benton in Scott County, where around 3 inches fell. Snowfall amounts elsewhere were from 1 to 2 inches. This snow and the accompanying flash freeze heavily impacted travel across the region. Despite relatively light traffic, there were numerous traffic mishaps. The Missouri Highway Patrol in southeast Missouri responded to 80 stranded motorists, 33 non-injury crashes, and 4 injury crashes during the winter storm.

A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 2 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event. Several accidents occurred across the county due to slick snow packed roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. Snowfall amount reports ranged from 1.5 inches 4 miles east of Goodman to 2.5 inches as reported by law enforcement 3 miles west northwest of Anderson. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of August 3rd, numerous thunderstorms developed across parts of eastern Missouri and west-central Illinois, triggered by strong surface heating ahead of a surging outflow boundary. These storms developed within a strongly unstable environment, featuring MLCAPE values ranging from 2500 to as much as 4000 J/kg and largely driven by the very warm and humid boundary layer. The initial round of storms struggled to become organized. Eventually cold pool interactions drove upscale growth into a more linear complex. Damaging winds, occasional large hail were reported as well as bursts of very heavy rain rates. By the evening of August 3rd, storms grew upscale, with additional rounds of thunderstorms developing upstream from this initial activity. The primary hazard transitioned from a damaging wind threat to a heavy rain and flash flooding threat, as storms were extremely efficient rain producers. Warm cloud depths were around 4.5km, along with anomalously high precipitable water values of 2 to 2.5 inches, and Corfidi vector orientations that supported westward back-building which enhanced the potential for locally heavy rain, both in terms of instantaneous rates (2+ inches per hour at times) and training of storms. By mid morning on August 4th, widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell, mainly along and south of I-70 in Missouri and along and east of I-55 in Illinois. Over the St. Louis metro area, as well as Fredericktown, MO, between 4 and 7 inches of rain fell. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 7 inches of rain fell in about a 2 to 3 hour period across northern portions of Madison County. Numerous creeks flooded, including Saline Creek in Fredericktown. Also, several rivers flooded including Little St. Francois River in Fredericktown, which rose 18.6 feet in 5 hours, cresting at 20.49 feet by 715 am. At least 21 people were rescued from the flood waters in and around Fredericktown. One person drove into flood waters on Highway Z on the east side of town and floated into the ditch. They were rescued and sustained no injuries. The flash flooding affected the Holmes Subdivision as well as other homes and businesses along Saline Creek and a creek along South Mine La Motte Avenue.

A surface low pressure center and its parent 500 mb shortwave moved south-southeast from the northern Plains across the middle Mississippi Valley. An area of snow in advance of these features produced an inch or two of accumulation during the late night and early morning hours. The snow readily accumulated on road surfaces due to the antecedent frigid temperatures the previous few days. Driving conditions were treacherous during the morning commute, but relatively light traffic on the observed Christmas holiday kept traffic mishaps less numerous than they otherwise might have been. The Missouri Highway Patrol responded to five crashes on Interstate 55 in Cape Girardeau County. The interstate was shut down at times due to the crashes. Three people were injured in one of the crashes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-DL-10 approximately 3 miles north northeast of Urbana reported 2.0 inches of snowfall. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 31 mph across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front swept through the region, with wind chills dropping to 20 to 30 below zero. These were the coldest wind chills observed in over 25 years across the region. Paducah, KY dropped down to a -21 wind chill, which was the coldest since February 3, 1996. The most extreme wind chills were from the evening of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd, but wind chills as late as the morning of December 24th were often below minus 10. Actual air temperatures only reached the single digits above zero on the 23rd, which followed morning lows around 5 below. Peak wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were observed during this event as well. The peak gust at Cape Girardeau was 44 mph on the evening of the 22nd. Some power outages were reported in Cape Girardeau, where a power line was down and a substation transformer caught fire. The extreme cold was indirectly responsible for a number of structure fires caused by supplemental heating sources, such as space heaters. There were numerous reports of frozen water pipes that burst and flooded homes and other buildings. In Cape Girardeau, an underground 6-inch water main burst, causing a loss of water pressure to a neighborhood. Another water main break occurred in Jackson. There were reports of frozen water meters in Cape Girardeau. On top of the extreme cold, one to three inches of snow fell. The highest amounts were generally along and south of a line from Van Buren in Carter County through Greenville in Wayne County to Benton in Scott County, where around 3 inches fell. Snowfall amounts elsewhere were from 1 to 2 inches. This snow and the accompanying flash freeze heavily impacted travel across the region. Despite relatively light traffic, there were numerous traffic mishaps. The Missouri Highway Patrol in southeast Missouri responded to 80 stranded motorists, 33 non-injury crashes, and 4 injury crashes during the winter storm.

A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 3 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event. Several accidents occurred across the county due to slick snow packed roads. Four minor to moderate injuries were reported with three of the accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of August 3rd, numerous thunderstorms developed across parts of eastern Missouri and west-central Illinois, triggered by strong surface heating ahead of a surging outflow boundary. These storms developed within a strongly unstable environment, featuring MLCAPE values ranging from 2500 to as much as 4000 J/kg and largely driven by the very warm and humid boundary layer. The initial round of storms struggled to become organized. Eventually cold pool interactions drove upscale growth into a more linear complex. Damaging winds, occasional large hail were reported as well as bursts of very heavy rain rates. By the evening of August 3rd, storms grew upscale, with additional rounds of thunderstorms developing upstream from this initial activity. The primary hazard transitioned from a damaging wind threat to a heavy rain and flash flooding threat, as storms were extremely efficient rain producers. Warm cloud depths were around 4.5km, along with anomalously high precipitable water values of 2 to 2.5 inches, and Corfidi vector orientations that supported westward back-building which enhanced the potential for locally heavy rain, both in terms of instantaneous rates (2+ inches per hour at times) and training of storms. By mid morning on August 4th, widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell, mainly along and south of I-70 in Missouri and along and east of I-55 in Illinois. Over the St. Louis metro area, as well as Fredericktown, MO, between 4 and 7 inches of rain fell. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large tree limbs in Ladue. In Warson Woods, a large tree was blown down onto a local road blocking it. In Kirkwood, a large tree was blown down on Sugar Creek Ridge Drive knocking down powerlines and poles.

Up to 4 inches fell in a short amount of time, especially in the Warson Woods, Frontenac, Kirkwood, Glendale, Sunset Hills, Crestwood, and Webster Groves areas.  Numerous roads were flooded including U.S. Highway 67 just south of Frontenac, Highway 100 (Manchester Road) east of McKnight Road due to flooding of Deer Creek, North Berry Road near Clif-Side Drive due to Shady Grove Creek flooding, and Big Bend Road near Sanders Drive due to Gravois Creek flooding. Several businesses sustained flood damage, especially along Manchester Road. Some vehicles were stranded in flood waters. No injuries were reported.

Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. A snowfall amount of 2.5 inches was reported at a CoCoRaHS station in Mansfield. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 37 mph across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front swept through the region, with wind chills dropping to 20 to 30 below zero. These were the coldest wind chills observed in over 25 years across the region. Paducah, KY dropped down to a -21 wind chill, which was the coldest since February 3, 1996. The most extreme wind chills were from the evening of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd, but wind chills as late as the morning of December 24th were often below minus 10. Actual air temperatures only reached the single digits above zero on the 23rd, which followed morning lows around 5 below. Peak wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were observed during this event as well. The peak gust at Cape Girardeau was 44 mph on the evening of the 22nd. Some power outages were reported in Cape Girardeau, where a power line was down and a substation transformer caught fire. The extreme cold was indirectly responsible for a number of structure fires caused by supplemental heating sources, such as space heaters. There were numerous reports of frozen water pipes that burst and flooded homes and other buildings. In Cape Girardeau, an underground 6-inch water main burst, causing a loss of water pressure to a neighborhood. Another water main break occurred in Jackson. There were reports of frozen water meters in Cape Girardeau. On top of the extreme cold, one to three inches of snow fell. The highest amounts were generally along and south of a line from Van Buren in Carter County through Greenville in Wayne County to Benton in Scott County, where around 3 inches fell. Snowfall amounts elsewhere were from 1 to 2 inches. This snow and the accompanying flash freeze heavily impacted travel across the region. Despite relatively light traffic, there were numerous traffic mishaps. The Missouri Highway Patrol in southeast Missouri responded to 80 stranded motorists, 33 non-injury crashes, and 4 injury crashes during the winter storm.

A strong arctic cold front moved through the region December 22nd.  The system brought a very rare combination of accumulating snowfall (generally 1-3) combined with very strong northwest winds (20-30 mph sustained, 35-45 mph gusts) which yielded sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Travel was extremely hazardous if not impossible during this time due to the falling (and blowing) snow. The bottom line is while snowfall amounts were not too substantial, the focus was on the high impacts to travel and the extremely dangerous cold/wind chills. Minimum wind chills Thursday night (12/22) into Friday morning (12/23) were in the -25 to - 40F range. For reference, these numbers were in the top 10 coldest observed at all three of our official climate stations (KSTL, KCOU, KUIN). Temperature wise, widespread below zero lows Friday morning (12/23) and were the coldest December temperatures seen in our area since December 23, 1989 (though about 10 degrees warmer). There were dozens of accidents across the county warning area during this event with a number of injuries ranging from minor to serious, but no deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 2 inches of snow fell. The snow combined with gusty northwest winds (35-45 mph gusts) created extremely hazardous travel with sporadic near-blizzard conditions during the heaviest snowfall. Road conditions deteriorated rapidly during this event. A couple of accidents occurred on hilly portions of I-44 due to several semi trucks jack knifing because of slick snow packed sections of highway. One person sustained serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of August 3rd, numerous thunderstorms developed across parts of eastern Missouri and west-central Illinois, triggered by strong surface heating ahead of a surging outflow boundary. These storms developed within a strongly unstable environment, featuring MLCAPE values ranging from 2500 to as much as 4000 J/kg and largely driven by the very warm and humid boundary layer. The initial round of storms struggled to become organized. Eventually cold pool interactions drove upscale growth into a more linear complex. Damaging winds, occasional large hail were reported as well as bursts of very heavy rain rates. By the evening of August 3rd, storms grew upscale, with additional rounds of thunderstorms developing upstream from this initial activity. The primary hazard transitioned from a damaging wind threat to a heavy rain and flash flooding threat, as storms were extremely efficient rain producers. Warm cloud depths were around 4.5km, along with anomalously high precipitable water values of 2 to 2.5 inches, and Corfidi vector orientations that supported westward back-building which enhanced the potential for locally heavy rain, both in terms of instantaneous rates (2+ inches per hour at times) and training of storms. By mid morning on August 4th, widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell, mainly along and south of I-70 in Missouri and along and east of I-55 in Illinois. Over the St. Louis metro area, as well as Fredericktown, MO, between 4 and 7 inches of rain fell. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding in the far northern portions of the county. Numerous roads were flooded including Highway 141. There were multiple water rescues on Highway 141, but no injuries were reported. Also, several water evacuations were performed at homes near the intersection of Romaine Creek Road and Konert Road. Twenty four home sustained minor flood damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen over the western portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month and started to spread east by the end of September. By the last week of September, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 58 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 11 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3), by the end of the month 12 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Significant Drought (D4) in 6 counties. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across most of the region through the month of September. The US Drought Monitor indicated the extreme northwestern part of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought with the remainder in Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1) Drought by the end of the month. The lack of rainfall received during the month provided no relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds showed significant impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing impacts as well. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow moving upper level disturbance that tracked eastward across the southern Plains into the lower and middle Mississippi Valley from the 2nd to the early morning of the 3rd. Several reports of flash flooding were received south of Highway 60 from Aurora and Cassville, and eastward toward Ava. Rainfall accumulations for the 24-hour period ending at 1 AM on 9/3/22 showed a large area of one to two inches along and east of a line from Joplin to Versailles, with greater than two inches along and south of a line from Monett to Mountain Grove. A few locations in Douglas County received over 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway P at the Douglas Branch was flooded and impassable after 4.05 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another period of excessive heat occurred across southeast Missouri. Heat indices peaked around 110 degrees on the 22nd, 23rd, and 24th. The peak heat index at Cape Girardeau was 115 on the 22nd, 109 on the 23rd, and 114 on the 24th. Another large 500 mb high became stubbornly persistent across the southern states, from the Four Corners region to the Southeast. Underneath this high, daily high temperatures were in the mid to upper 90's. Overnight lows were in the lower to mid 70's.

Drought conditions began to worsen over the western portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month and started to spread east by the end of September. By the last week of September, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 58 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 11 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3), by the end of the month 12 counties were in (D2) Severe drought or greater with Significant Drought (D4) in 6 counties. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across most of the region through the month of September. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northwestern corner of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder in Moderate (D1) Drought to Abnormally Dry (D0) by the end of the month. The lack of rainfall received during the month provided no relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds showed significant impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing impacts as well. Dry conditions allowed for wild fires to occur, though no large fires impacted the county. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow moving upper level disturbance that tracked eastward across the southern Plains into the lower and middle Mississippi Valley from the 2nd to the early morning of the 3rd. Several reports of flash flooding were received south of Highway 60 from Aurora and Cassville, and eastward toward Ava. Rainfall accumulations for the 24-hour period ending at 1 AM on 9/3/22 showed a large area of one to two inches along and east of a line from Joplin to Versailles, with greater than two inches along and south of a line from Monett to Mountain Grove. A few locations in Douglas County received over 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway H at the North Fork Spring River was closed due top flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms affected areas along and south of Highway 60 from the afternoon of the 23rd to the early morning of the 25th. The strongest storms developed during the afternoon of the 24th along Highway 60, and produced damaging wind gusts over 60 mph. Rainfall accumulations over two inches occurred over portions of south central Missouri from Mansfield to Winona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree were reported down on Highway 76 between County Roads 149 and 156.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived thunderstorm cluster moved east-southeast during the morning hours across  the mid Mississippi Valley. These storms moved into an axis of greater instability over southeast Missouri, contributing to an uptick in intensity. Wind damage was observed in portions of Wayne and Stoddard Counties. Rather weak wind shear limited the duration and areal extent of the severe storms. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east-southeastward from the upper Mississippi Valley across the Great Lakes. The storms occurred near an associated surface cold front that moved southeastward across the Ohio Valley and southern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten trees were blown down across roads in western Wayne County from Piedmont to the Williamsville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several loosely organized clusters of thunderstorms produced localized strong wind gusts and isolated hail. The lack of greater vertical wind shear limited organization of the storms. A cold front moved south-southeast from the Plains and Great Lakes, preceded by multiple thunderstorm outflow boundaries. The storms reached their peak intensity during the late afternoon and early evening, when the diurnal peak of instability was reached.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Highway 114 near Miner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the upper level pattern shifted from southwest to northwest flow over the central U.S. and an upper level ridge expanded over the Plains, meso-scale convective systems (MCS) moved over the region during the overnight and early morning hours each day. The most impactful storms occurred during the early morning of the 8th, when storms produced damaging wind gusts from the Lake of the Ozarks region southward to near Interstate 44. Damage to trees, powerlines and roofs occurred, and two people were injured near Kaiser in Camden County when a tree fell on their RV. At least one inch of rain fell with each event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell over on a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one hour before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-PL-21 approximately 5 miles east southeast of Aldrich reported 1.0 inches of snowfall. Other snow reports across the county varied from 0.5 inches northwest of Dunnegan to 1.0 inch in Morrisville and Halfway. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 41 mph across the county resulting in periodic blowing snow. A vehicle crash with two injuries five miles east of Bolivar on Missouri Highway 32 after the driver went off the roadway and overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of December. By the last week of December, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 23 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 7 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3), by the end of the month 3 counties were in (D2) Severe drought with no counties in Extreme Drought (D3) or greater. The Extreme Drought (D3) had improved falling to Sever Drought (D2) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across portions of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of December. The US Drought Monitor indicated the southwestern half of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought to Abnormally Dry (D0). Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Estimated monetary crop losses will be included in future a report when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and dangerous with reduced visibility during the snowfall along with gusty winds over 30 mph.  Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-JS-30 approximately 4.5 miles east of Carthage reported 2.4 inches of snowfall. The JLN ASOS reported 0.03 inches of flat ice accumulation due to the freezing drizzle. Other snow reports across the county varied from 1.5 inches southwest of Carl Junction to 2.0 inches just north of Joplin. Due to extreme cold conditions and the ineffectiveness of treatment options, roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 43 mph across the county resulting in periodic blowing snow.  Impacts were further magnified by occurring during the morning rush hour and being the first significant snow of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month of December. By the last week of December, the US Drought Monitor had designated around 23 percent of the area as being in moderate drought (D1) to significant drought (D4). Across the Ozarks, the month started with 7 counties in Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3), by the end of the month 3 counties were in (D2) Severe drought with no counties in Extreme Drought (D3) or greater. The Extreme Drought (D3) had improved falling to Sever Drought (D2) by the end of the month across the Missouri Ozarks. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across portions of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of December. The US Drought Monitor indicated the northwestern corner of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought.  Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Estimated monetary crop losses will be included in future a report when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across Dade County by the end of December. The US Drought Monitor indicated that moderate (D1) drought conditions remained at the end of the month after several rainfall events helped ease drought conditions below severe (D2) drought levels. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops.  Estimated monetary crop losses included in this months report are for drought conditions through December 31st, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass entered the region on the morning of December 22nd, plunging temperatures to below zero. Strong winds combined with the cold temperatures yielded wind chills between -25 and -40 below zero from roughly 10 am on 12/22 to noon on 12/23. A Wind Chill Warning was in effect across the entire CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass sent temperatures below zero along with strong winds. Minimum wind chills across the region generally ranged from -30 to -40 degrees between roughly 10 am on 12/22 to noon on 12/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts up to an inch in Clark and Scotland Counties. Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas. Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area. Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, with low visibilities. In addition, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -30 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported snowfall accumulations of 0.8 inches 3 miles east northeast of Charlie Heath Memorial Park . Winds increased significantly with gusts of 40 to 50 MPH after the snow fell leading to blowing and drifting snow.  An estimated wind chill of -23 was reported at Kahoka.

Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHS observer MO-LW-9 approximately 9 miles northwest of Miller reported 2.5 inches of snowfall. Four separate accidents occurred as a result of the slick conditions, one of which included a tractor trailer overturning on Interstate 44 that resulted in a three hour delay. A total of 6 vehicles were damaged and 8 injuries were reported across the four separate accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid morning into afternoon hours, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile or less along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 3 inches of snow, leading to slick roads, numerous vehicle crashes and slide offs across the area, and power outages across portions of the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -37 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one to two hours before transitioning to snow. A public report from 2 miles northwest of Northview reported 2.9 inches of snowfall. Additionally, COOP observer MRFM7 approximately 2 miles north northeast of Marshfield reported 2.1 inches of snowfall. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 37 mph across the county resulting in periodic blowing snow. A vehicle crash with one moderate injury occurred  on Missouri Highway B approximately 7 miles north of Rogersville after the driver travelled off the side of the road and struck a few trees.

Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 30 mph across the county resulting in periodic blowing snow. A pile up of 20 to 30 vehicles, including tractor trailers and passenger vehicles, occurred at the 142 mile marker in the eastbound lane of Interstate 44 around 1 PM CST. Six people were injured, with the interstate remaining closed for nearly six hours.
